_disclaimer: Content is for general informational purposes only. Not legal advice. Laws change — always verify with a licensed attorney in your state.

Alabama.quick_summary: Courts apply the best interests of the child standard. Joint legal custody is common; sole custody is awarded when joint arrangements are not in the child's best interests.
Alabama.custody_standard: Alabama courts apply the 'best interests of the child' standard when determining custody. Factors include the child's age, the parents' ability to meet the child's needs, any history of domestic violence or abuse, the child's relationship with each parent, and the child's preference if the child is of sufficient maturity.
Alabama.custody_types: Alabama recognizes both legal custody (decision-making authority over education, healthcare, religious upbringing) and physical custody (where the child lives). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is common; joint physical custody requires both parents to be able to cooperate. Sole custody may be granted when joint custody is not in the child's best interests.
Alabama.modification_rules: A custody order can be modified if there has been a material change in circumstances since the last order. The change must be substantial enough to justify a custody modification and the proposed change must serve the child's best interests. Courts examine whether circumstances have materially changed and whether modification benefits the child.
Alabama.relocation_rules: A parent seeking to relocate must provide the other parent with at least 45 days advance written notice. The non-relocating parent may file an objection. The court will consider whether the move is in good faith, the impact on the child's relationship with the other parent, and how visitation can be maintained. The burden is on the relocating parent to show the move is in the child's best interests.
Alabama.enforcement_options: Alabama courts can enforce custody orders through contempt proceedings, which may result in fines or jail time. Courts may also award makeup visitation, attorney fees, and modify custody as a result of repeated violations. Law enforcement can assist in returning a child to the custodial parent. The Uniform Child Custody Jurisdiction and Enforcement Act (UCCJEA) allows for interstate enforcement.
Alabama.mediation_requirements: Alabama courts may order mediation in contested custody cases. Mediation is encouraged but not universally mandatory. Some circuits have local rules requiring mediation before a contested hearing. Courts can refer parties to mediation at any stage of the proceeding to help resolve disputes about custody and visitation.

Alaska.quick_summary: Courts use the best interests standard with no gender preference. Joint legal and shared physical custody are both available; a two-year waiting period applies before most modifications.
Alaska.custody_standard: Alaska courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child, considering factors such as the physical, emotional, mental, religious, and social needs of the child; the capability and desire of each parent to meet those needs; the child's preference if of sufficient age; any special needs of the child; the stability of the home environment; and any domestic violence, child abuse, or neglect.
Alaska.custody_types: Alaska provides for legal custody (decision-making) and physical custody (residence). Both can be sole or shared. Shared custody can be either shared legal, shared physical, or both. Courts encourage parents to work out parenting plans. If parents cannot agree, the court will determine the custody arrangement based on the child's best interests.
Alaska.modification_rules: To modify custody, a parent must show a substantial change in circumstances that warrants revisiting the arrangement. The court then considers whether modification is in the child's best interests. Alaska has a two-year waiting period before custody modifications can be requested, with exceptions for domestic violence, substance abuse issues, or the child's preference.
Alaska.relocation_rules: A parent wishing to relocate must provide 30 days written notice if the move would significantly change the child's contact with the other parent. The non-relocating parent can object. The court will weigh the reasons for the move, the impact on the child's relationship with the non-moving parent, and proposed accommodations for maintaining that relationship.
Alaska.enforcement_options: Custody orders may be enforced through contempt of court proceedings, resulting in fines or jail time. Courts may also award attorney fees and modify the custody arrangement. Emergency orders are available if the child is in danger. The UCCJEA governs interstate enforcement.
Alaska.mediation_requirements: Alaska statutes encourage the use of mediation in custody disputes. Courts may order parties to participate in mediation before a hearing on custody. Mediation is generally confidential and is not mandatory in every case, but is strongly promoted as a means to resolve parenting disputes without litigation.

Arizona.quick_summary: Arizona uses "legal decision-making" and "parenting time" rather than traditional custody terms. Courts presume joint legal decision-making is in the child's best interests absent domestic violence.
Arizona.custody_standard: Arizona uses the term 'legal decision-making' for what other states call legal custody, and 'parenting time' for visitation/physical custody. Courts decide based on the best interests of the child, evaluating parental relationships, each parent's past, present, and potential future relationships with the child, the child's adjustment to home, school, and community, mental and physical health of all parties, and any coercion or duress used to obtain an agreement.
Arizona.custody_types: Arizona recognizes sole and joint legal decision-making. For parenting time, courts aim to maximize each parent's time with the child unless contrary to the child's best interests. Arizona law presumes joint legal decision-making unless there is evidence of domestic violence. The court can award one parent final decision-making authority over specific matters like education or healthcare.
Arizona.modification_rules: A parent must wait one year after entry of a custody order before seeking modification, unless there is serious endangerment to the child. After one year, modification requires showing a substantial and continuing change in circumstances. The court will again apply the best interests standard.
Arizona.relocation_rules: A parent must provide 45 days written notice before relocating more than 100 miles or out of state. The other parent may object, and the court will hold a hearing. Arizona courts consider the relocation's impact on the parenting plan and the child's relationship with the non-relocating parent. The relocating parent bears the burden of proving the move is in the child's best interests.
Arizona.enforcement_options: Arizona courts can hold a parent in contempt for violating parenting time orders. Remedies include make-up parenting time, civil penalties, attorney fees, community service, or in serious cases, jail time. Courts may also modify the parenting plan if violations are persistent. The UCCJEA governs interstate matters.
Arizona.mediation_requirements: Arizona courts frequently order mediation in contested custody cases. In Maricopa County, mediation through the conciliation court is commonly required before a contested hearing. Many Arizona courts have mandatory parent information programs and may require parenting conferences or mediation as a prerequisite to litigation.

California.quick_summary: California prioritizes the child's health, safety, and welfare and favors frequent contact with both parents. Domestic violence creates a presumption against joint custody.
California.custody_standard: California courts determine child custody based on the health, safety, and welfare of the child as the primary standard. Courts strongly favor arrangements that assure the child frequent and continuing contact with both parents. Domestic violence is a significant factor, and courts presume that joint custody is detrimental if a parent has been found to have committed domestic violence.
California.custody_types: California recognizes legal custody (decision-making over education, healthcare, religious upbringing) and physical custody (residence). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is common and encourages both parents to share major decisions. Joint physical custody means the child spends substantial time with both parents. Courts may award primary physical custody to one parent with visitation rights to the other.
California.modification_rules: To modify a final custody order, a parent must show a significant change of circumstances. However, for temporary orders or those recently issued, courts apply a 'best interests' test without the change of circumstances requirement. The move-away context has specific rules, and the parent with primary custody bears the burden of showing the move is appropriate.
California.relocation_rules: A parent with joint physical custody who wants to move must give 45 days advance written notice and may need to seek court approval if the move would change the current custody arrangement. California courts will consider the extent to which the move affects the existing custodial arrangement and the child's relationship with the non-moving parent. The court applies the 'best interests' standard.
California.enforcement_options: California courts can enforce custody orders through contempt proceedings, resulting in fines or imprisonment. Make-up visitation may be ordered. The court may modify custody arrangements as a result of willful violations. California has a Child Abduction Unit and law enforcement assistance for parental abduction cases. The UCCJEA applies for interstate enforcement.
California.mediation_requirements: California requires mandatory mediation before any contested custody hearing. This is provided through Family Court Services at no charge to the parties. In some counties (like Los Angeles), the mediator may make a recommendation to the court if the parties cannot reach an agreement. Mediation is confidential except for the mediator's report in recommending counties.

Colorado.quick_summary: Colorado allocates parental responsibilities — both decision-making and parenting time — based on the child's best interests. Joint decision-making is common absent a history of domestic violence.
Colorado.custody_standard: Colorado uses the term 'parental responsibilities' and courts allocate both decision-making and parenting time based on the best interests of the child. Factors include the parents' wishes, the child's preferences, the child's relationship with each parent and siblings, the child's adjustment to home, school, and community, the mental and physical health of all individuals, the ability of each parent to encourage love and contact with the other parent, and any evidence of child abuse or domestic violence.
Colorado.custody_types: Colorado distinguishes between 'decision-making responsibility' (similar to legal custody) and 'parenting time' (similar to physical custody). Both can be allocated solely or jointly. Joint decision-making is common unless there is a history of domestic violence or abuse. Parenting time schedules are detailed in a parenting plan that outlines each parent's time with the child.
Colorado.modification_rules: Colorado requires a two-year waiting period before modifying parenting time unless circumstances endanger the child. After two years, substantial change in circumstances is required. Modification of decision-making also requires a change in circumstances and that the modification serves the child's best interests.
Colorado.relocation_rules: A parent with primary parenting time must provide 91 days written notice before relocating with the child. The non-relocating parent may object. The court will consider the reasons for the relocation, the quality of the relationships involved, the impact on the child, and whether a modified schedule can preserve the non-relocating parent's relationship with the child.
Colorado.enforcement_options: Colorado courts enforce parenting time and decision-making orders through contempt proceedings. Remedies include makeup parenting time, civil fines, attorney fees, or modification of the existing order. Courts may also issue bench warrants for parents who repeatedly violate orders. Interstate enforcement is governed by the UCCJEA.
Colorado.mediation_requirements: Colorado courts may order mediation in custody disputes. Some judicial districts require mandatory mediation before setting a contested hearing. Colorado also offers the option of parenting coordinators or decision-makers for ongoing disputes. The Colorado Office of Dispute Resolution maintains a roster of approved mediators.

Florida.quick_summary: Florida presumes shared parental responsibility (joint legal custody) in most cases. Time-sharing schedules are individually tailored; equal time-sharing is a common starting point.
Florida.custody_standard: Florida uses the term 'parental responsibility' and 'time-sharing' instead of custody. Courts determine arrangements based on the best interests of the child. Florida has 20 statutory factors including the demonstrated capacity of each parent to facilitate a close parent-child relationship, the division of parental responsibilities, any history of domestic violence, the geographical viability of the plan, and the moral fitness of the parents.
Florida.custody_types: Florida provides for 'shared parental responsibility' (similar to joint legal custody) which is the default, and 'sole parental responsibility' in exceptional cases. Time-sharing schedules determine where and when the child spends time with each parent. Florida recognizes equal time-sharing as a starting point, but this can be adjusted based on best interests factors and the statutory 20-factor test.
Florida.modification_rules: Florida requires a substantial, material, and unanticipated change in circumstances to modify a time-sharing schedule or parental responsibility. Additionally, the modification must be in the child's best interests. This high standard protects stability for children.
Florida.relocation_rules: Florida requires parents with time-sharing to provide notice and obtain consent or court approval for relocations more than 50 miles from the primary residence. Parents must file a petition with the court, and both parties will present evidence regarding the potential impact on the child. The relocating parent bears the burden of demonstrating the move is in the child's best interests.
Florida.enforcement_options: Florida courts enforce time-sharing orders through contempt, which can result in fines, community service, or jail time. Courts may order makeup time-sharing, require the violating parent to pay attorney fees, or modify the time-sharing plan. Law enforcement can assist in returning a child to the appropriate parent. The UCCJEA applies for interstate situations.
Florida.mediation_requirements: Florida requires mandatory mediation in most contested family law cases, including custody disputes, unless waived for good cause such as domestic violence. Mediation must be completed before a contested hearing. Florida certified family mediators conduct sessions, and courts may order parenting coordination as an additional service for high-conflict cases.

Georgia.quick_summary: Courts award custody using the best interests standard. Children aged 14 and older may select a preferred parent, and the court must give that preference serious weight.
Georgia.custody_standard: Georgia courts award custody based on the best interests of the child. Key factors include each parent's love, affection, and emotional ties with the child; the willingness of each parent to support the other's relationship with the child; each parent's ability to provide for the child's basic needs; the home environment of each parent; the stability of family life for the child; each parent's knowledge and familiarity with the child; and any evidence of domestic violence or substance abuse.
Georgia.custody_types: Georgia recognizes legal custody (decision-making over education, healthcare, religious upbringing) and physical custody (where the child lives). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody allows both parents to share major decisions. Physical custody can be primary (child primarily lives with one parent) or joint (child splits time between both parents). Georgia courts may also consider the child's preference if the child is 14 or older.
Georgia.modification_rules: To modify a Georgia custody order, a parent must show a material change in circumstances affecting the welfare of the child since the last order. If the child is 14 or older, the child has the right to select the parent with whom they prefer to live, subject to the court's approval. For children between 11 and 13, the court considers their preference but it is not binding.
Georgia.relocation_rules: A parent with primary physical custody must provide 30 days advance written notice to the other parent and the court before relocating to another state or more than 50 miles from the other parent within Georgia. The non-relocating parent can petition the court to prevent the move or modify custody. The court will evaluate whether the move is in the child's best interests.
Georgia.enforcement_options: Custody orders in Georgia can be enforced through contempt of court proceedings. A parent who willfully violates a custody order may face fines, attorney fees, and in serious cases, jail time. Courts may also modify custody as a consequence of repeated violations. Georgia law allows for the filing of a 'writ of habeas corpus' to enforce custody rights.
Georgia.mediation_requirements: Georgia courts encourage mediation and may order it in contested custody cases. Many circuits require parties to attend mediation before a final contested hearing. The Georgia Office of Dispute Resolution certifies family mediators. Some counties have local rules mandating mediation, and parenting plans are typically required in all custody cases.

Illinois.quick_summary: Illinois allocates decision-making responsibilities and parenting time separately under the Parental Responsibilities Reform Act. Joint decision-making is common when parents can cooperate.
Illinois.custody_standard: Illinois uses the term 'allocation of parental responsibilities' which replaced the traditional custody terminology. Courts allocate responsibilities based on the best interests of the child. Factors include the child's wishes, the parents' wishes, the child's adjustment to home, school, and community, the mental and physical health of all parties, the ability of parents to cooperate, and any history of domestic violence or abuse.
Illinois.custody_types: Illinois allocates 'significant decision-making responsibilities' (similar to legal custody) and 'parenting time' (similar to physical custody). Significant decisions include education, healthcare, extracurricular activities, and religious upbringing. Both parenting time and decision-making can be shared or allocated primarily to one parent.
Illinois.modification_rules: Illinois has a two-year waiting period before modifications can be requested unless the child's present environment seriously endangers the child. After two years, modification requires showing a substantial change of circumstances and that modification serves the child's best interests. For parenting time modifications (not decision-making), courts apply a lower threshold.
Illinois.relocation_rules: A parent with majority parenting time who wants to relocate with the child must provide 60 days written notice. If the other parent objects, the relocating parent must file a petition with the court. Relocations within Illinois of 25 miles or more (for the Chicago area) or 50 miles or more (other areas), or any relocation to another state, require notice or court approval.
Illinois.enforcement_options: Illinois courts enforce parenting time orders through contempt proceedings. Remedies include makeup parenting time (up to twice the denied time), attorney fees, and modification of the parenting plan. Courts can issue civil warrants for parents who repeatedly deny parenting time. The UCCJEA governs interstate enforcement.
Illinois.mediation_requirements: Illinois courts may order mediation in contested custody and parenting time cases. Mediation is not uniformly mandatory across all counties, but many circuit courts have local rules requiring it. Cook County and many other counties strongly encourage or require mediation before contested hearings. Domestic violence victims may be exempt from mandatory mediation requirements.

Michigan.quick_summary: Courts use 12 statutory best-interest factors to award custody. Joint legal custody is presumed beneficial; physical custody arrangements are tailored to meet the individual child's needs.
Michigan.custody_standard: Michigan courts award custody based on the best interests of the child using 12 statutory factors. These include emotional ties between the child and each parent, the capacity of each parent to give the child love and guidance, the capacity of each parent to provide for material needs, the length of time the child has lived in a stable environment, the permanence of the family unit, the moral fitness of the parents, the mental and physical health of the parties, the child's school record, the child's preference if of an appropriate age, the willingness of each parent to facilitate visitation, and any evidence of domestic violence.
Michigan.custody_types: Michigan recognizes legal custody (decision-making authority) and physical custody (residential placement). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is presumed to be in the child's best interests unless one parent is deemed unfit. Joint physical custody means the child spends substantial time with both parents. Michigan courts may award primary physical custody to one parent with parenting time (visitation) to the other.
Michigan.modification_rules: To modify a Michigan custody order, a parent must first show proper cause or a change of circumstances. If shown, the court then considers whether the change warrants revisiting the best interests factors. Michigan also recognizes a 'established custodial environment,' which, if changed, requires a higher burden of proof (clear and convincing evidence).
Michigan.relocation_rules: Michigan requires a parent with custody of a minor child to obtain court approval or the other parent's consent before moving the child's domicile more than 100 miles from the original location. Courts will apply factors specific to relocation, including the reasons for the proposed move, the other parent's reasons for opposing, and the child's relationships with each parent.
Michigan.enforcement_options: Michigan courts enforce custody orders through contempt proceedings, which can result in fines or incarceration. Courts may order makeup parenting time and attorney fees. If a parent repeatedly violates orders, the court may modify custody arrangements. Law enforcement can assist with enforcement. The UCCJEA governs interstate cases.
Michigan.mediation_requirements: Michigan courts may refer custody disputes to mediation. Many circuits require mediation before contested hearings. Michigan also uses Friend of the Court services, which include mediation and investigation services in custody disputes. Parenting time disputes are commonly handled through the Friend of the Court office, which provides free mediation services.

New York.quick_summary: Courts apply the best interests standard with no presumption for joint or sole custody. Parental fitness, the child's relationship with each parent, and household stability are key considerations.
New York.custody_standard: New York courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. There are no specific statutory factors, but courts consider parental fitness, the home environment, the child's relationship with each parent, the quality of each parent's parenting, the parent's ability to provide for the child's emotional, intellectual, and physical needs, and any history of domestic violence or substance abuse. New York courts also consider the preference of older children.
New York.custody_types: New York recognizes legal custody (decision-making authority) and physical custody (where the child lives). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is common but requires the parents to be able to communicate and cooperate. Joint physical custody means substantial time with both parents. Courts can craft highly individualized arrangements based on the specific family's circumstances.
New York.modification_rules: To modify a New York custody order, a parent must show a substantial change in circumstances since the last order and that modification would serve the child's best interests. Courts also consider stability and continuity for the child. Changes such as a parent's relocation, a significant change in a parent's lifestyle, or the child's own changing needs may qualify as substantial changes.
New York.relocation_rules: New York has no specific statute requiring advance notice of relocation. However, courts balance the custodial parent's interest in moving with the non-custodial parent's interest in maintaining a relationship with the child. The relocating parent must demonstrate that the move is in the child's best interests based on factors like the reason for the move, the impact on the child's relationship with the other parent, and quality of life improvements.
New York.enforcement_options: New York courts enforce custody orders through family offense proceedings and contempt citations. Violations can result in modification of custody, fines, jail time, and attorney fee awards. Law enforcement can assist in enforcing custody orders. Courts may issue pickup orders if a child is improperly retained. Interstate enforcement is governed by the UCCJEA.
New York.mediation_requirements: New York courts may refer custody matters to mediation through court-annexed programs. Mediation is available through the Office of Court Administration's Community Dispute Resolution Centers. While not uniformly mandatory, many judges encourage or order mediation to help parents resolve disputes. Mediation is voluntary in cases involving domestic violence.

North Carolina.quick_summary: Courts determine custody based on the best interests and welfare of the child. Joint or sole legal and physical custody are both available and tailored to each family's circumstances.
North Carolina.custody_standard: North Carolina courts determine custody based on the best interests and welfare of the child. While there are no specific statutory factors (unlike some states), courts consider the fitness of each parent, the quality of each parent's relationship with the child, each parent's ability to provide for the child, stability and continuity in the child's life, the child's relationship with siblings, any history of domestic violence or abuse, and the preference of older children.
North Carolina.custody_types: North Carolina provides for legal custody (decision-making authority) and physical custody (where the child lives). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody allows both parents to share major decisions regarding the child. Joint physical custody means the child spends substantial time with both parents. Courts craft custody orders tailored to each family's unique circumstances.
North Carolina.modification_rules: North Carolina allows modification of custody orders when there has been a substantial change in circumstances affecting the welfare of the child. The court then applies the best interests standard. Examples of substantial changes include a significant change in a parent's lifestyle, a new relationship that affects the child, relocation, or the child's changing needs.
North Carolina.relocation_rules: North Carolina does not have a specific relocation statute, but custody orders typically include provisions regarding relocation. If a parent wants to relocate and it would affect the custody arrangement, the other parent may petition the court for modification. Courts evaluate the best interests of the child considering the proposed move.
North Carolina.enforcement_options: North Carolina courts enforce custody orders through contempt of court. Violations can result in fines, attorney fees, makeup visitation, and modification of the custody arrangement. Courts can also issue bench warrants in serious cases. Interstate enforcement is governed by the UCCJEA.
North Carolina.mediation_requirements: North Carolina requires mandatory mediation in all contested child custody cases before a hearing. This is provided through Custody and Visitation Mediation Programs associated with the courts. Mediation is confidential and must be completed before a contested custody hearing, except in cases involving domestic violence or substance abuse where a waiver may be granted.

Ohio.quick_summary: Courts apply the best interests standard. Shared parenting (joint custody) requires a court-approved parenting plan; one parent may be designated as the residential parent for school enrollment purposes.
Ohio.custody_standard: Ohio courts award custody based on the best interests of the child. Relevant factors include the wishes of the child's parents, the child's wishes if they are of an appropriate age, the child's interaction and interrelationship with parents and siblings, the child's adjustment to home, school, and community, the mental and physical health of all parties, the parent more likely to honor and facilitate visitation rights, whether child support is being paid, and any history of domestic violence or drug abuse.
Ohio.custody_types: Ohio recognizes 'legal custody' (decision-making authority) and 'physical custody' (residential placement). Both can be sole or joint (called 'shared parenting' in Ohio). Shared parenting requires both parents to file a shared parenting plan; if only one parent requests it, the court may still order it if it is in the child's best interests. Courts can also designate a 'residential parent' for school purposes.
Ohio.modification_rules: Ohio requires a change in circumstances to modify a custody order; however, if the parents have shared parenting, modifications are slightly more flexible. The change must affect the child, and modification must be in the child's best interests. Ohio courts apply different standards depending on whether there is shared parenting or primary custody.
Ohio.relocation_rules: Ohio requires parents with residential custody to notify the court and the other parent when planning to relocate. The non-residential parent may file a motion for reallocation of parental rights and responsibilities. Courts consider the reason for the move, the impact on the child's relationship with the other parent, and proposed arrangements for maintaining contact.
Ohio.enforcement_options: Ohio courts enforce custody orders through contempt proceedings, which can result in fines or jail time. Courts may order makeup parenting time and attorney fee awards. Courts can also modify custody arrangements if violations are persistent. The Ohio UCCJEA governs interstate enforcement.
Ohio.mediation_requirements: Ohio courts may order mediation in contested custody cases. Many Ohio courts have mediation programs affiliated with their domestic relations divisions. While not universally required, mediation is strongly encouraged and frequently ordered as a prerequisite to contested hearings. Some counties have specific local rules requiring mediation before trial.

Pennsylvania.quick_summary: Courts apply 16 statutory best-interest factors to determine custody. Primary, shared, and partial physical custody are all available; no presumption favors joint or sole arrangements.
Pennsylvania.custody_standard: Pennsylvania courts award custody based on the best interests of the child, applying 16 statutory factors. These include the parties' potential to encourage close and continuing contact between the child and the other party; the present and past abuse; the parental duties performed by each party; the need for stability and continuity in the child's education, family life, and community life; the availability of extended family; the child's sibling relationships; well-reasoned preference of the child based on maturity and judgment; and any evidence of domestic violence.
Pennsylvania.custody_types: Pennsylvania provides for 'legal custody' (decision-making about important decisions) and 'physical custody' (where the child lives). Each can be sole or shared. Shared legal custody is common when parents can cooperate. Physical custody arrangements include primary physical custody (child primarily with one parent), shared physical custody (child spends substantial time with both), and partial physical custody or supervised physical custody.
Pennsylvania.modification_rules: Pennsylvania courts can modify custody orders when there has been a substantial change in circumstances. Courts will then apply the best interests standard. Unlike some states, Pennsylvania does not have a waiting period for modifications. However, courts do consider the child's stability and continuity when evaluating changes.
Pennsylvania.relocation_rules: Pennsylvania requires a parent seeking to relocate to provide notice to the other parent and all parties with custody rights. The notice must be served 60 days in advance and include specific information about the proposed move. The other parent can object by filing a counter-affidavit. If there is an objection, the court holds a hearing and determines whether relocation is in the child's best interests.
Pennsylvania.enforcement_options: Pennsylvania courts enforce custody orders through contempt proceedings. A parent who violates a custody order may be held in contempt, resulting in fines, imprisonment, makeup custody time, and attorney fees. Courts can also modify custody arrangements as a consequence. Interstate enforcement is governed by the UCCJEA.
Pennsylvania.mediation_requirements: Pennsylvania courts may order mediation in contested custody cases. Many Pennsylvania counties have mediation programs for custody disputes. Some courts require parties to attend a custody conciliation conference before scheduling a contested hearing, which serves a similar function to mediation. Courts have broad discretion to order mediation at any stage of proceedings.

Texas.quick_summary: Texas presumes joint managing conservatorship (joint legal custody) is in the child's best interests. One parent typically receives the exclusive right to designate the child's primary residence.
Texas.custody_standard: Texas uses the terms 'conservatorship' (custody) and 'possession and access' (visitation). Courts determine arrangements based on the best interests of the child. Factors include the child's physical and emotional needs, each parent's abilities, stability of home environment, any domestic violence or abuse history, the child's relationship with each parent, and the parents' ability to place the child's needs above their own.
Texas.custody_types: Texas provides for 'joint managing conservatorship' (similar to joint legal custody) and 'sole managing conservatorship.' Joint managing conservatorship is the legal presumption and means both parents share rights and duties, though not necessarily equal possession time. Sole managing conservatorship is awarded when joint is contrary to the best interests of the child, typically when there is domestic violence or abuse.
Texas.modification_rules: To modify a Texas custody order, you must show a material and substantial change in circumstances affecting the child or a conservator since the last order, or that the child is at least 12 years old and has expressed a preference to the court. The modification must also be in the child's best interests. Texas has a one-year waiting period for most modifications.
Texas.relocation_rules: Texas custody orders often include a geographic restriction that limits where the child can live (often the county where the suit was filed plus contiguous counties). A parent wanting to move outside this area must petition the court or get consent from the other parent. The court will evaluate whether the move is in the child's best interests and how visitation can be maintained.
Texas.enforcement_options: Texas courts enforce custody orders through motions for enforcement and contempt, which can result in fines, community service, jail time, and makeup possession time. Courts can also modify custody arrangements as a result of violations. Texas has specific enforcement statutes that allow for attorney fee awards. The UCCJEA governs interstate enforcement.
Texas.mediation_requirements: Texas courts may order mediation in contested custody (conservatorship) cases. Many Texas courts routinely order mediation before setting contested cases for trial. Texas law permits courts to impose sanctions on a party who fails to participate in mediation in good faith. Mediation agreements in Texas are generally binding and difficult to revoke once signed.

Virginia.quick_summary: Courts use 10 statutory best-interest factors to determine custody. Both joint and sole legal and physical custody are available; no particular arrangement is presumed or preferred.
Virginia.custody_standard: Virginia courts determine child custody and visitation based on the best interests of the child. Statutory factors include the age and physical and mental condition of the child and parents; the relationship between each parent and child; the needs of the child; the role played by each parent in the upbringing of the child; the propensity of each parent to actively support the child's contact with the other parent; the willingness to resolve disputes by mutual agreement; the reasonable preference of the child if the court deems the child to be of reasonable intelligence and maturity; and any history of family abuse.
Virginia.custody_types: Virginia provides for legal custody (decision-making authority) and physical custody (residential arrangement). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is favored when parents can cooperate. Joint physical custody means the child spends substantial time with both parents. Courts may award primary physical custody to one parent with visitation to the other. Courts can also order supervised visitation if necessary for the child's safety.
Virginia.modification_rules: Virginia allows modification of custody orders upon a showing of a material change in circumstances and that modification would serve the child's best interests. Material changes include significant changes in a parent's life circumstances, relocation, a parent's remarriage, or the child's changing needs.
Virginia.relocation_rules: Virginia does not have a specific relocation statute, but courts impose relocation restrictions in custody orders. A parent wishing to relocate must either obtain the other parent's consent or court approval. Courts consider the impact of the proposed move on the child's relationship with both parents and whether the relocation is in the child's best interests.
Virginia.enforcement_options: Virginia courts enforce custody orders through civil contempt proceedings. Violations can result in fines, attorney fees, makeup visitation, and modification of custody. Courts can also hold parents criminally liable for custodial interference. Law enforcement can assist with enforcement. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Virginia.mediation_requirements: Virginia courts encourage mediation and may order parties to attend mediation in contested custody cases. Virginia has a robust network of court-affiliated mediation programs. Some Virginia courts require mediation before a contested hearing. Mediation is generally not required in cases involving domestic violence. Courts may also appoint a guardian ad litem to represent the child's interests.

Washington.quick_summary: Washington uses a parenting plan framework covering residential schedules and major decisions. Courts focus on the strength of the child's relationships with each parent and any history of domestic violence.
Washington.custody_standard: Washington uses the term 'parenting plan' to govern custody arrangements. Courts determine plans based on the best interests of the child. Factors include the strength of the child's relationships with each parent, each parent's past and potential future performance of parenting functions, emotional climate in each home, the child's cultural background and community ties, and any history of domestic violence or abuse.
Washington.custody_types: Washington establishes a residential schedule detailing when the child is with each parent, and allocates decision-making authority for major decisions. Decision-making can be mutual (joint) or reserved for one parent for specified areas. Residential arrangements range from primary residence with one parent to equal time-sharing, based on what serves the child's best interests.
Washington.modification_rules: Washington allows modification of parenting plans when there is a substantial change in circumstances of the child or a parent. Courts will not consider modifications unless the child has been integrated into the family of the petitioner with consent, or the child's present environment is detrimental. The court applies the best interests standard after finding the threshold is met.
Washington.relocation_rules: Washington requires 60 days written notice before a parent with primary residential time relocates with a child. The other parent can object. If there is an objection, the relocating parent must file a petition, and the court will evaluate the move. Washington courts consider the relative impact of relocation versus non-relocation on the child's development.
Washington.enforcement_options: Washington courts enforce parenting plans through contempt of court. Violations can result in fines, attorney fees, makeup residential time, and modification of the parenting plan. Courts can also require violating parents to attend parenting classes. Law enforcement can assist in enforcing residential schedules. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Washington.mediation_requirements: Washington courts may order mediation in parenting plan disputes. Many Washington counties have dispute resolution programs affiliated with their family courts. While not universally mandatory, mediation is strongly encouraged. Washington has a network of dispute resolution centers that provide mediation services. Courts may impose conditions requiring mediation before any modification hearing.

Indiana.quick_summary: Courts apply the best interests standard with specific statutory factors. Joint legal custody is available when parents demonstrate the ability to cooperate; a two-year waiting period applies before most modifications.
Indiana.custody_standard: Indiana courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. Statutory factors include the child's age and sex; the wishes of the parents and the child if the child is at least 14 years old; the child's adjustment to home, school, and community; the mental and physical health of all parties; findings or testimony from the child's teacher, counselor, or physician; and any history of domestic violence or child abuse.
Indiana.custody_types: Indiana recognizes legal custody (decision-making authority over education, healthcare, and religious upbringing) and physical custody (where the child lives). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody means both parents share major decisions. Physical custody can be primary (child lives primarily with one parent) or joint (child spends substantial time with both parents). Courts encourage cooperative parenting arrangements.
Indiana.modification_rules: Indiana requires a showing of a substantial change in circumstances to modify a custody order. The change must relate to matters affecting the child's welfare. Indiana has a two-year waiting period after the entry of a custody order before a modification can be filed, with exceptions for situations involving domestic violence, child abuse, or other urgent circumstances affecting the child's safety.
Indiana.relocation_rules: Indiana requires a parent who plans to relocate to provide the other parent with at least 30 days advance written notice. The notice must include the new address, the reason for the relocation, and a proposed revised parenting time schedule. The other parent may object to the relocation, and the court will determine whether relocation is in the child's best interests and how visitation will be maintained.
Indiana.enforcement_options: Indiana courts enforce custody orders through contempt of court proceedings. A parent who violates a custody or parenting time order may face fines, makeup parenting time, attorney fee awards, or incarceration. Courts may also modify the custody order as a consequence of repeated violations. Interstate enforcement is handled under the UCCJEA.
Indiana.mediation_requirements: Indiana courts may order mediation in contested custody cases. Many Indiana courts encourage or require mediation before setting a contested hearing. Some circuits have local rules mandating mediation or a settlement conference in custody disputes. Courts have broad discretion to refer parties to mediation at any stage of the proceeding to help resolve parenting disputes.

Louisiana.quick_summary: Louisiana presumes joint custody is preferred and requires a joint custody implementation plan. Courts weigh 12 statutory factors and consider the child's preference if the child is of sufficient age.
Louisiana.custody_standard: Louisiana courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. Louisiana is unique as the only state with a Civil Law tradition. Statutory factors under Louisiana Civil Code Article 134 include the love, affection, and emotional ties between each party and the child; the capacity of each party to give the child love, affection, and spiritual guidance; the capacity of each party to provide material needs; the length of time the child has lived in a stable, adequate environment and the desirability of maintaining continuity; the permanence of the family unit; the moral fitness of each party; the mental and physical health of each party; the home, school, and community history of the child; and the reasonable preference of the child if the court deems the child to be of sufficient age.
Louisiana.custody_types: Louisiana recognizes joint custody and sole custody. Joint custody is the preferred arrangement and means both parents share legal authority and responsibility for major decisions affecting the child. Under joint custody, one parent may be designated the domiciliary parent with whom the child primarily resides. The non-domiciliary parent has visitation rights and participates in major decisions. Sole custody is granted when joint custody would be detrimental to the child's best interests.
Louisiana.modification_rules: To modify a Louisiana custody order, a parent must show a material change in circumstances that has occurred since the last order and that the proposed modification is in the best interests of the child. Louisiana courts apply a higher burden when a party seeks to change the domiciliary parent. The Bergeron standard (based on a landmark Louisiana case) requires proof that the advantages to the child outweigh the inherent risk of change.
Louisiana.relocation_rules: Louisiana requires a parent seeking to relocate more than 75 miles from the other parent, or out of state, to provide at least 60 days written notice before the intended move. The non-relocating parent may object. If there is an objection, the court holds a hearing and considers factors including the reason for the relocation, the quality of the relationship between the child and each parent, the impact on visitation, and whether the move is in the child's best interests.
Louisiana.enforcement_options: Louisiana courts enforce custody and visitation orders through rule to show cause or contempt proceedings. A parent who willfully disobeys a custody order may face fines, attorney fees, makeup visitation, and incarceration. Courts may also modify custody as a consequence of persistent violations. Louisiana law allows for criminal prosecution of parental kidnapping. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Louisiana.mediation_requirements: Louisiana courts may refer contested custody cases to mediation. Many Louisiana district courts encourage or require mediation before setting contested custody matters for trial. Some districts have local rules mandating pre-trial mediation or conciliation conferences. Mediation is generally voluntary but strongly encouraged as a means of resolving disputes without litigation. Courts retain full authority to approve or reject any mediated agreement.

Massachusetts.quick_summary: Courts determine custody using the best interests standard with no presumption favoring either parent. Shared legal custody is common; out-of-state relocation requires court approval or written consent.
Massachusetts.custody_standard: Massachusetts courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. While there is no specific statutory list of factors, courts consider the parents' willingness to support the child's relationship with the other parent, the child's relationship with each parent, each parent's ability to provide for the child's needs, the stability of each home environment, the child's adjustment to home, school, and community, the child's preference if of sufficient age and maturity, and any history of domestic violence or abuse.
Massachusetts.custody_types: Massachusetts recognizes legal custody (decision-making authority over education, healthcare, and religious upbringing) and physical custody (where the child lives). Both can be sole or shared. Shared legal custody means both parents participate in major decisions. Shared physical custody means the child spends significant time with both parents. The court may award sole legal and physical custody to one parent if the other is unfit or if joint arrangements are not in the child's best interests.
Massachusetts.modification_rules: Massachusetts allows modification of custody orders upon a showing of a material and substantial change in circumstances since the entry of the order. The court then applies the best interests standard to determine whether modification is warranted. Changes such as a parent's relocation, significant changes in a parent's lifestyle, or the child's changing needs may qualify as material changes.
Massachusetts.relocation_rules: Massachusetts has specific rules governing parental relocation under Massachusetts General Laws c. 208, §30. A parent with physical custody who wishes to move out of state must either obtain the other parent's written consent or court approval. The court applies a two-part test: whether the move is in good faith for a legitimate purpose, and whether the move is in the child's best interests considering the impact on the child's relationship with the non-moving parent.
Massachusetts.enforcement_options: Massachusetts courts enforce custody and visitation orders through contempt proceedings. A parent who violates a custody order may face fines, attorney fees, makeup visitation, and incarceration. Courts may also modify custody as a consequence of repeated violations. In serious cases, courts can issue a writ of habeas corpus or issue an order for the child's return. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Massachusetts.mediation_requirements: Massachusetts courts strongly encourage mediation in custody disputes. The Massachusetts Probate and Family Court has a Mediation Program that provides low-cost mediation services. Some courts require parties to attend an orientation or parent education program before a contested custody hearing. While mediation is not universally mandatory, many judges encourage or order mediation as a prerequisite to trial.

Nevada.quick_summary: Nevada presumes joint physical and legal custody is in the child's best interests. Courts use 9 statutory factors; evidence of domestic violence or abuse can rebut the presumption.
Nevada.custody_standard: Nevada courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. Nevada law creates a presumption that joint custody is in the best interests of the child, unless there is evidence of domestic violence or abuse. Statutory factors include the love, affection, and emotional ties between each parent and the child; the ability of each parent to give the child love, affection, and guidance; the child's relationship with siblings; the mental and physical health of each parent; the physical, developmental, and emotional needs of the child; the nature of the relationship of the child with each parent; the ability of each parent to cooperate; and the child's preference if the child is of sufficient age and maturity.
Nevada.custody_types: Nevada recognizes legal custody (decision-making authority) and physical custody (residential placement). Under Nevada's strong preference for joint custody, joint physical custody means the child spends approximately equal time with both parents. Joint legal custody means both parents share major decisions regarding the child. Courts may award primary physical custody to one parent if joint physical custody would not serve the child's best interests. Nevada law requires courts to set parenting time that ensures each parent has a meaningful relationship with the child.
Nevada.modification_rules: Nevada requires a parent seeking to modify custody to show a substantial change of circumstances affecting the welfare of the child. If the existing order provides for joint physical custody, the parent must show changed circumstances affecting the best interests of the child. Nevada courts are generally reluctant to modify custody arrangements that are working well for the child.
Nevada.relocation_rules: Nevada requires a parent with physical custody who wishes to relocate outside of Nevada, or to a location more than 100 miles from the other parent within Nevada, to provide at least 45 days written notice. The non-relocating parent may object. If there is an objection, the court holds a hearing and evaluates the relocation's impact on the child's relationship with the other parent and whether the move is in the child's best interests.
Nevada.enforcement_options: Nevada courts enforce custody orders through contempt of court proceedings. Violations can result in fines, attorney fees, makeup parenting time, modification of the custody arrangement, and incarceration in serious cases. Courts may also issue a civil standby order allowing law enforcement to assist in exercising parenting time. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Nevada.mediation_requirements: Nevada courts frequently order mediation in contested custody cases. Many Nevada family courts have on-site mediation programs. In Clark County (Las Vegas), the Family Mediation Center provides mediation services for custody and visitation disputes. Nevada courts may require parents to attempt mediation before a contested custody hearing. Mediation is confidential, and courts typically require parties to participate in good faith.

New Jersey.quick_summary: Courts evaluate custody using 14 statutory best-interest factors. Joint legal custody is strongly preferred; physical custody allocation is based on the child's needs and parental fitness.
New Jersey.custody_standard: New Jersey courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. New Jersey has 14 statutory factors under N.J.S.A. 9:2-4, including the parents' ability to agree, communicate, and cooperate in matters relating to the child; the parents' willingness to accept custody and any history of unwillingness; the interaction and relationship of the child with its parents and siblings; the history of domestic violence; the safety of the child and the safety of either parent from physical abuse; the preference of the child when of sufficient age and capacity; the needs of the child; the stability of the home environment offered; the quality and continuity of the child's education; the fitness of the parents; the geographical proximity of the parents' homes; the extent and quality of the time spent with the child prior to or subsequent to separation; the parents' employment responsibilities; and the age and number of the children.
New Jersey.custody_types: New Jersey recognizes legal custody (decision-making authority over major decisions) and physical custody (residential placement). Both can be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is strongly preferred and means both parents share major decisions regarding the child's education, healthcare, and religious upbringing. Physical custody can be primary (child lives primarily with one parent) or shared (child spends substantial time with both). Courts may also craft detailed parenting time schedules tailored to the family's needs.
New Jersey.modification_rules: New Jersey allows modification of custody orders upon a showing of a substantial change in circumstances. The court then applies the best interests standard to determine whether modification is warranted. Factors such as a parent's relocation, changes in a parent's work schedule, remarriage, or the child's changing needs and preferences may constitute substantial changes.
New Jersey.relocation_rules: New Jersey has specific statutes governing parental relocation. A parent with primary custody seeking to relocate out of state must obtain the other parent's consent or court approval. Under the Bisbing standard, the relocating parent must show that the move is in the best interests of the child. Courts evaluate the impact on the child's relationship with the non-moving parent, the reason for the proposed move, and the parenting plan that would allow the child to maintain a meaningful relationship with both parents.
New Jersey.enforcement_options: New Jersey courts enforce custody orders through contempt of court proceedings. A parent who violates a custody order may face fines, attorney fees, makeup parenting time, and incarceration. Courts may also modify custody arrangements as a consequence of repeated violations. New Jersey has specific enforcement statutes that provide remedies for violations. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
New Jersey.mediation_requirements: New Jersey courts strongly encourage mediation in contested custody cases. The New Jersey Courts offer an Early Settlement Panel program and a Complementary Dispute Resolution program, both of which may include mediation. Many vicinage courts require parties to attempt mediation before a contested custody hearing. Parent education programs are frequently required in custody disputes. New Jersey courts have broad discretion to order mediation at any stage of the proceeding.

Oklahoma.quick_summary: Courts apply the best interests standard; neither parent is favored on the basis of sex. Joint legal custody is preferred when parents can cooperate; domestic violence bars joint custody awards.
Oklahoma.custody_standard: Oklahoma courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. Oklahoma statute provides that in awarding custody, the court shall consider, among other things, the physical, mental, and moral welfare of the child; the child's preference if the child is of sufficient age and has expressed a preference; the willingness and ability of each parent to facilitate and support the child's relationship with the other parent; and any history of domestic violence or abuse. Oklahoma does not prefer either parent on the basis of sex.
Oklahoma.custody_types: Oklahoma recognizes legal custody (decision-making authority) and physical custody (where the child lives). Both can be sole or joint. Joint custody means both parents share rights and responsibilities regarding major decisions affecting the child. The court may designate a primary physical custodian with whom the child primarily resides. Joint custody requires the parents to be able to cooperate and communicate regarding the child's welfare. Sole custody may be awarded when joint custody is not in the child's best interests.
Oklahoma.modification_rules: Oklahoma allows modification of custody orders upon a showing of a substantial and material change of circumstances since the last order. The change must be permanent or ongoing rather than temporary. The court then applies the best interests standard to determine whether modification is in the child's best interests. Oklahoma courts are cautious about frequent custody modifications and prioritize stability for the child.
Oklahoma.relocation_rules: Oklahoma requires a parent with custody of a child who plans to relocate to a principal residence more than 75 miles from the other parent to provide at least 60 days advance written notice. The non-relocating parent may object by filing a motion with the court within 30 days of receiving notice. The court then determines whether the relocation is in the child's best interests, considering factors including the reason for the move, the impact on the child's relationship with the other parent, and proposed modifications to the parenting plan.
Oklahoma.enforcement_options: Oklahoma courts enforce custody orders through contempt of court proceedings. A parent who willfully disobeys a custody order may face fines, attorney fees, makeup parenting time, and incarceration. Courts may also modify custody arrangements as a consequence of persistent violations. Oklahoma law allows for criminal charges in cases of parental kidnapping or custodial interference. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Oklahoma.mediation_requirements: Oklahoma courts may order mediation in contested custody cases. Many Oklahoma district courts encourage or require mediation before setting contested custody hearings. The Oklahoma Dispute Resolution Act supports the use of mediation in family law matters. Courts may also appoint a parenting coordinator to assist parents in resolving disputes about the implementation of a parenting plan without returning to court.

Arkansas.quick_summary: Arkansas courts decide custody under the best interests of the child standard. State law favors frequent and continuing contact with both parents when that can be done safely, and joint custody is now expressly favored in many cases.
Arkansas.custody_standard: Arkansas courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. Judges look at the child's safety, stability, emotional ties with each parent, each parent's ability to meet the child's needs, and any history of domestic violence, substance abuse, or neglect. Arkansas law also directs courts to consider whether each parent will foster the child's relationship with the other parent. No parent is preferred based on gender.
Arkansas.custody_types: Arkansas recognizes legal custody (decision-making authority) and physical custody (where the child lives). Both can be sole or joint. Joint custody is favored by statute when parents can cooperate and the arrangement serves the child's best interests. Courts can still award primary physical custody to one parent with parenting time to the other when shared arrangements are not workable.
Arkansas.modification_rules: A custody order can be modified when there has been a material change in circumstances since the last order. The change must be important enough to justify reopening custody, and the requested modification must be in the child's best interests. Courts often look at instability, repeated conflict, safety concerns, or major changes in the child's needs. Arkansas courts emphasize continuity and do not modify custody lightly.
Arkansas.relocation_rules: Arkansas relocation disputes are handled through case law rather than a single notice statute. When a primary custodian wants to move, courts evaluate whether the move is being made in good faith and whether it is consistent with the child's best interests. Judges consider the reasons for the move, the effect on the child's relationship with the other parent, and whether a revised schedule can preserve meaningful contact. A parent seeking to move should expect court review if the relocation will significantly affect parenting time.
Arkansas.enforcement_options: Arkansas courts can enforce custody and visitation orders through contempt proceedings. Remedies may include attorney fees, makeup parenting time, fines, and in serious cases jail time. Courts may also modify custody if one parent repeatedly interferes with the other parent's court-ordered time. Interstate enforcement is available under the UCCJEA.
Arkansas.mediation_requirements: Arkansas courts may order mediation in contested custody cases. Mediation is not mandatory statewide in every case, but many judges use it before a final hearing. Local practice can vary by county and judicial district. Mediation may be waived or limited when domestic violence or coercive control is a concern.

Connecticut.quick_summary: Connecticut uses the best interests of the child standard and encourages shared parental responsibility when appropriate. Courts often enter detailed parenting plans covering decision-making and schedules, but safety concerns can outweigh any preference for cooperation.
Connecticut.custody_standard: Connecticut courts decide custody according to the best interests of the child. Statutory factors include each parent's ability to understand and meet the child's needs, the child's temperament and developmental needs, the child's relationship with each parent, the willingness of each parent to facilitate contact with the other, and any history of abuse or domestic violence. The court may consider the child's wishes if the child is mature enough. No single factor controls the outcome.
Connecticut.custody_types: Connecticut recognizes legal custody and physical custody, and either may be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is common when parents can communicate and make major decisions together. Physical custody can be shared or primarily with one parent, depending on the child's needs and the practical realities of the parents' schedules. Courts often require a parenting plan that spells out holidays, decision-making, and transitions.
Connecticut.modification_rules: Custody can be modified when there has been a material change in circumstances or when a final order no longer serves the child's best interests. The parent seeking change must show why the existing order should be revisited. Courts focus on whether the change affects the child's welfare in a meaningful way. Stability matters, so modification requests are examined carefully.
Connecticut.relocation_rules: Connecticut relocation cases are decided under the child's best interests standard. A parent planning to move with the child should expect court review if the move will substantially affect the existing parenting schedule. Judges look at the reason for the move, the feasibility of preserving the child's relationship with the other parent, and the likely impact on the child. Courts can adjust parenting plans to address travel and long-distance contact.
Connecticut.enforcement_options: Connecticut courts enforce custody orders through motions for contempt and other post-judgment remedies. Judges may order compliance, award attorney fees, require makeup parenting time, or impose sanctions for willful violations. Repeated interference can also support later custody modification. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Connecticut.mediation_requirements: Connecticut family courts strongly encourage mediation and other family services in contested custody cases. Judicial branch family relations staff often meet with parents before a contested hearing. Courts can order custody mediation or evaluation when it may help resolve parenting disputes. Cases involving abuse or intimidation may be handled differently for safety reasons.

Delaware.quick_summary: Delaware awards custody based on the child's best interests using statutory factors. Courts distinguish between legal custody, residential placement, and visitation, and they tailor schedules to the child's needs rather than assuming one arrangement fits every family.
Delaware.custody_standard: Delaware courts apply the best interests of the child standard. Statutory factors include the wishes of the parents and child, the child's interaction with parents and household members, the child's adjustment to home, school, and community, the mental and physical health of everyone involved, compliance with parental rights and responsibilities, and any history of domestic violence. The court may also consider criminal history when relevant to the child's safety. The goal is a practical arrangement that protects the child and supports stable parenting.
Delaware.custody_types: Delaware recognizes legal custody, residential placement, and visitation. Legal custody concerns major decisions such as education and healthcare and may be sole or joint. Residential placement addresses where the child lives and can be primary with one parent or shared between both. Courts often use detailed schedules and may separate decision-making authority from residential time if that better serves the child.
Delaware.modification_rules: A custody or placement order may be modified when there is a real and substantial change in circumstances and the requested change is in the child's best interests. Delaware places weight on finality, so not every parenting disagreement justifies reopening an order. Courts look for changes affecting the child's welfare, safety, or stability. Emergency relief is available when a child's well-being is at immediate risk.
Delaware.relocation_rules: Delaware relocation disputes are analyzed through the best interests framework and the effect on contact with the other parent. A parent who plans to move with the child should expect to give notice and, if the move is contested, seek court approval. Judges consider the reason for the move, the impact on schooling and stability, and whether meaningful contact with the nonmoving parent can be preserved. The court can revise placement and visitation terms when a move is approved.
Delaware.enforcement_options: Delaware Family Court can enforce custody and placement orders through contempt, makeup contact, attorney fees, and other remedial orders. Persistent violations may also justify changes to the existing parenting arrangement. In urgent cases, the court can issue pick-up or return orders. Interstate enforcement is handled under the UCCJEA.
Delaware.mediation_requirements: Delaware Family Court regularly uses mediation and other court-connected dispute resolution services in custody matters. Many contested cases are screened for mediation before a final hearing. Mediation is not appropriate in every case, especially where domestic violence or coercive conduct is present. Parents may also be referred to parenting classes or evaluations.

Hawaii.quick_summary: Hawaii applies the best interests of the child standard and uses parenting plans to structure custody and visitation. Joint legal custody is common when parents can cooperate, but the court will depart from shared arrangements when safety or stability concerns are present.
Hawaii.custody_standard: Hawaii courts decide custody based on the best interests of the child. Judges consider the child's physical, emotional, and educational needs, the child's relationship with each parent, the parents' ability to communicate and cooperate, and any history of family violence or substance abuse. Courts also consider continuity in caregiving and school placement. The child's preference may matter when the child is mature enough to express a reasoned view.
Hawaii.custody_types: Hawaii recognizes legal custody and physical custody, each of which may be sole or joint. Legal custody covers major decisions about education, healthcare, and religion. Physical custody addresses where the child lives and how parenting time is shared. Courts often require a parenting plan that lays out schedules, transportation, holidays, and communication rules.
Hawaii.modification_rules: A custody order may be changed when there has been a material change in circumstances and modification is in the child's best interests. The parent asking for change must show more than ordinary parenting friction. Courts look for changes in safety, stability, caregiving ability, or the child's needs. Hawaii courts generally protect continuity unless there is a sound reason to alter the order.
Hawaii.relocation_rules: Hawaii does not rely on a single relocation formula, but a move that substantially affects the current parenting plan will usually require court review. Judges weigh the purpose of the move, the effect on the child's relationship with the other parent, travel burdens, and whether an adjusted schedule can preserve frequent and meaningful contact. The child's best interests remain the controlling standard. Parents should expect scrutiny when a move would separate the child from regular contact with the other parent.
Hawaii.enforcement_options: Hawaii courts enforce custody and visitation orders through contempt proceedings and related family court remedies. Judges may order attorney fees, make-up time, counseling, or other corrective relief when a parent willfully violates an order. Repeated interference may support later modification. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Hawaii.mediation_requirements: Hawaii family courts make regular use of mediation in custody disputes. Many cases are referred to mediation, parenting education, or other family court services before a contested trial. Mediation is encouraged as a way to build workable parenting plans, but it may be limited when abuse or intimidation is present. Local practice can vary by island and circuit.

Idaho.quick_summary: Idaho courts use the best interests of the child standard and do not presume one parent should receive custody because of sex. Courts can award sole or joint custody, and they focus heavily on stability, caregiving ability, and the child's relationship with each parent.
Idaho.custody_standard: Idaho courts decide custody based on the best interests of the child. Factors include the wishes of the child's parents, the child's wishes when the child is mature enough, the child's relationship with each parent and siblings, adjustment to home and school, the character and circumstances of the people involved, and the need to promote continuity and stability. Domestic violence and substance abuse are important considerations. The court's focus is practical parenting, not parental entitlement.
Idaho.custody_types: Idaho recognizes legal and physical custody, either of which may be sole or joint. Joint custody is available when parents can reasonably cooperate and the arrangement serves the child. Courts may divide decision-making differently from physical time if that best fits the family. Parenting plans often address schooling, medical decisions, holidays, and transportation.
Idaho.modification_rules: An Idaho custody order may be modified when there has been a substantial and material change in circumstances and the requested change would serve the child's best interests. Courts look for meaningful changes affecting the child's welfare rather than minor disagreements. Stability remains important, especially after a final order has been in place for some time. Emergency relief is available when the child faces immediate danger.
Idaho.relocation_rules: Idaho relocation cases are governed by the best interests standard and the terms of the existing parenting plan. A parent who wants to move in a way that substantially affects the other parent's time will typically need either consent or court approval. Judges consider the good-faith reason for the move, the impact on the child's relationship with the nonmoving parent, and whether an alternate schedule can preserve meaningful contact. The court may revise custody or visitation if relocation is approved.
Idaho.enforcement_options: Idaho courts enforce custody orders through contempt, compensatory parenting time, attorney fees, and other remedial orders. Judges may also issue writs or pick-up orders when a child is wrongfully withheld. Repeated violations can influence future custody decisions. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Idaho.mediation_requirements: Idaho courts often encourage or require mediation in contested custody cases. Many judicial districts use family court services or local mediation rosters before a final hearing. Mediation is intended to help parents build workable parenting plans and reduce litigation. Cases involving domestic violence may be screened out or handled with additional safety measures.

Iowa.quick_summary: Iowa generally favors continuing contact with both parents and often orders joint legal custody. Physical care may be shared or primary with one parent, depending on whether the parents can communicate and meet the child's needs.
Iowa.custody_standard: Iowa courts use the best interests of the child standard. Judges consider stability, each parent's caregiving history, the ability of the parents to communicate respectfully, the child's emotional and developmental needs, and any history of domestic abuse. Iowa also looks at which arrangement is most likely to encourage the child to have healthy contact with both parents. Safety concerns can overcome any preference for shared parenting.
Iowa.custody_types: Iowa recognizes legal custody and physical care. Joint legal custody is common and means both parents share major decisions about education, health care, and religion. Physical care can be placed primarily with one parent or shared between both, depending on the facts. Courts require a parenting plan in contested cases and may approve detailed shared-care schedules when they are workable.
Iowa.modification_rules: Iowa applies a demanding standard for custody modification. A parent usually must show a substantial change in circumstances that was not contemplated when the prior order was entered and must also show the ability to provide superior care. Courts are reluctant to disturb stable arrangements without a strong reason. Parenting-time changes alone may be easier to obtain than a full change in physical care.
Iowa.relocation_rules: Iowa does not use a single relocation notice formula for every case, but a move that significantly affects the parenting schedule will usually require notice and court review. Judges consider the reason for the move, the impact on the child's relationship with the other parent, and whether a revised schedule can preserve meaningful contact. The child's best interests remain the controlling question. Courts may adjust physical care or visitation if a relocation changes what is practical.
Iowa.enforcement_options: Iowa courts enforce custody and visitation orders through contempt, makeup time, attorney fees, and related family court remedies.
