Willful interference with parenting time can carry sanctions and may influence later custody decisions. Courts can also use the UCCJEA to enforce orders across state lines. Emergency relief is available if a child is being wrongfully withheld.
Iowa.mediation_requirements: Iowa courts frequently use mediation in family law cases. Many custody disputes are referred to mediation before a final trial, especially when the parents need help developing a parenting plan. Mediation may be waived or handled differently when domestic abuse is present. Parent education requirements may also apply.

Kansas.quick_summary: Kansas awards legal custody and residency based on the child's best interests. Joint legal custody is common, but residential placement depends on the child's needs, parental cooperation, and any safety concerns.
Kansas.custody_standard: Kansas courts decide custody based on the best interests of the child. Factors include each parent's involvement in care, the child's adjustment to home and school, the willingness of each parent to support the child's relationship with the other, and the mental and physical health of the people involved. Courts also consider domestic violence and patterns of controlling behavior. The child's preferences may matter when they are reasoned and age-appropriate.
Kansas.custody_types: Kansas recognizes legal custody, residency, and parenting time. Legal custody may be joint or sole, with joint legal custody being common when parents can share major decisions. One parent may be designated the residential parent while the other receives parenting time, or the parents may share residential time more evenly. Courts may divide decision-making responsibilities if that better serves the child.
Kansas.modification_rules: To modify custody in Kansas, a parent must generally show a material change in circumstances and that the requested change is in the child's best interests. Courts look for changes that genuinely affect the child's welfare, not simply normal conflict between parents. Stability matters, especially once a final order has been working. Kansas courts can also make temporary adjustments when immediate concerns arise.
Kansas.relocation_rules: Kansas requires notice when a parent intends to move the child to a different residence in a way that substantially affects the parenting plan. If the move is contested, the court will review whether it is consistent with the child's best interests. Judges look at the reason for the move, the distance involved, and whether the child's relationship with the other parent can be preserved. A move can lead to changes in residency or parenting time when necessary.
Kansas.enforcement_options: Kansas courts enforce custody and parenting time orders through contempt proceedings and related family court remedies. Judges may order makeup time, attorney fees, fines, and other corrective relief for willful violations. Repeated denial of contact can also support later modification. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Kansas.mediation_requirements: Kansas courts regularly use mediation, case management, and parenting coordination in custody disputes. Many contested cases are referred to mediation before trial. Courts may decline mediation or use safeguards when domestic violence is alleged. Local judicial district practice can shape how mediation is scheduled and required.

Kentucky.quick_summary: Kentucky law creates a presumption of joint custody and equal parenting time, but that presumption can be rebutted when the facts show a different arrangement is better for the child. Courts still decide each case under the child's best interests and pay close attention to domestic violence and caregiving history.
Kentucky.custody_standard: Kentucky courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. Judges consider the parents' wishes, the child's wishes when mature enough, the child's relationship with parents and household members, adjustment to home and school, mental and physical health, and any history of domestic violence. Kentucky's statutory presumption in favor of joint custody and equal parenting time does not override safety concerns. The court must still enter the arrangement that best serves the child.
Kentucky.custody_types: Kentucky recognizes legal custody and parenting time. Legal custody may be joint or sole, and the court can allocate parenting time equally or primarily to one parent. Because of the statutory presumption, many cases begin with joint custody and substantial time with both parents as the baseline. Courts can depart from that baseline when parental conflict, distance, work schedules, or abuse concerns make another arrangement better.
Kentucky.modification_rules: Custody may be modified when there has been a change in circumstances and the requested change serves the child's best interests. Kentucky law also contains stricter standards for some motions filed soon after the prior decree. Courts take stability seriously and require more than ordinary parenting disputes. When a child's safety or emotional development is at risk, the court may intervene more quickly.
Kentucky.relocation_rules: Kentucky does not rely on a single relocation notice statute for every custody case, but a move that materially affects parenting time can be brought before the court. Judges consider the reason for the move, the practical effect on the child's relationship with the other parent, and whether an alternate schedule can preserve meaningful contact. Existing parenting plans or local orders may include notice provisions. Relocation can result in changes to custody or time-sharing when needed.
Kentucky.enforcement_options: Kentucky courts enforce custody and timesharing orders through contempt, attorney fees, makeup time, and other family court remedies. A parent who repeatedly interferes with court-ordered contact may face sanctions and may lose credibility in later custody proceedings. Courts can also use emergency orders when a child is being wrongfully withheld. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Kentucky.mediation_requirements: Kentucky courts often encourage mediation in custody disputes, and some family courts routinely refer cases before trial. Mediation is not appropriate in every case, especially where domestic violence makes safe participation difficult. Parenting classes and other family court services may also be ordered. Local practice varies across counties and judicial circuits.

Maine.quick_summary: Maine allocates parental rights and responsibilities using the child's best interests as the controlling standard. Courts may award shared or sole parental rights, but they focus on workable parenting arrangements rather than defaulting to equal time.
Maine.custody_standard: Maine courts decide parental rights and responsibilities based on the best interests of the child. Statutory factors include the child's age and relationships, each parent's ability to give love and guidance, the child's adjustment to home and school, the stability of each home, and any history of abuse or domestic violence. Courts also consider which parent is more likely to encourage frequent and continuing contact with the other parent when safe. The child's preference may be considered if the child is mature enough.
Maine.custody_types: Maine uses the terms parental rights and responsibilities, residence, and contact. Rights and responsibilities can be shared or allocated primarily to one parent, and the court may divide specific decision-making areas if needed. A child may live primarily with one parent or spend substantial time with both. Parenting plans in Maine often address decision-making, holidays, transportation, and communication.
Maine.modification_rules: A final parental rights and responsibilities order may be modified when there has been a substantial change in circumstances and the modification serves the child's best interests. Courts require a meaningful shift in facts, not just continuing disagreement. Stability remains an important value once an order has been entered. Temporary relief can be granted when there is an urgent safety concern.
Maine.relocation_rules: Maine relocation disputes are analyzed under the best interests standard and the terms of the existing parenting order. A parent planning to move in a way that significantly affects contact should expect court review if the move is contested. Judges consider the reason for the move, the impact on schooling and relationships, and whether a revised schedule can preserve meaningful contact. A relocation can justify changes to residence or contact terms.
Maine.enforcement_options: Maine courts enforce custody and contact orders through contempt and other post-judgment remedies. Judges may order attorney fees, compensatory contact, and other corrective relief for willful violations. Repeated interference can support later modification of parental rights and responsibilities. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Maine.mediation_requirements: Maine family courts frequently use mediation in parental rights and responsibilities cases. Many contested cases are referred to mediation early in the process. Mediation is encouraged to help parents develop child-focused parenting plans, but courts can bypass or limit it where domestic abuse is an issue. Additional family matter services may also be ordered.

Maryland.quick_summary: Maryland uses the best interests of the child standard with strong emphasis on caregiving history, fitness, and the child's relationships. Courts can award joint or sole legal and physical custody, but there is no automatic presumption that joint custody is best in every case.
Maryland.custody_standard: Maryland courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. Factors drawn from case law include parental fitness, character and reputation, the desire of the natural parents and any agreements between them, the child's ties to school and community, the child's preference if mature enough, and each parent's willingness to share custody and communicate. Domestic violence and child safety are central concerns. The analysis is highly fact-specific and focuses on what arrangement is workable for the child.
Maryland.custody_types: Maryland recognizes legal custody and physical custody, and each may be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is possible when parents can communicate and make major decisions together. Physical custody can be shared or primarily with one parent, with access to the other parent. Courts may award one form jointly and the other solely depending on what the evidence supports.
Maryland.modification_rules: A Maryland custody order may be modified when there has been a material change in circumstances and the requested change is in the child's best interests. The court first decides whether circumstances have changed enough to reopen custody. If so, it performs a full best-interests analysis. Stability matters, so courts expect a real change affecting the child's welfare before altering a final order.
Maryland.relocation_rules: Maryland does not rely on a single relocation formula, but a move that disrupts the current custody schedule will often require notice and court review. Judges consider the reason for the move, the effect on the child's relationship with the other parent, travel burdens, and whether a realistic revised schedule can preserve meaningful contact. Best interests remain controlling. A contested relocation can result in custody or visitation modification.
Maryland.enforcement_options: Maryland courts enforce custody orders through contempt, attorney fee awards, makeup access, and related equitable remedies. In serious cases the court may order the child's return or consider modification. Repeated interference with access can weigh heavily against the violating parent in later proceedings. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Maryland.mediation_requirements: Maryland circuit courts often refer custody and visitation disputes to mediation. Some counties make mediation routine before a final merits hearing, while others screen cases individually. Mediation may be limited or bypassed where abuse or coercion is present. Courts may also order custody evaluations or parent education.

Minnesota.quick_summary: Minnesota decides custody using detailed best-interest factors and emphasizes the child's safety, stability, and relationship with both parents. Courts may award joint or sole legal and physical custody, but they do not treat equal time as automatic.
Minnesota.custody_standard: Minnesota courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child under statutory factors. Judges consider the child's physical, emotional, cultural, spiritual, and developmental needs; each parent's history of participating in care; the child's relationship with each parent and siblings; the effect of proposed arrangements on the child's stability; and any history of domestic abuse. The court also evaluates each parent's ability to support the child's relationship with the other parent when safe. The analysis is intentionally child-centered rather than parent-centered.
Minnesota.custody_types: Minnesota recognizes legal custody and physical custody, and each may be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is common in cases without significant abuse or decision-making conflict. Joint physical custody is possible but not presumed. Courts often adopt parenting plans that define decision-making, schedules, dispute resolution, and communication rules.
Minnesota.modification_rules: Minnesota generally requires a significant change in circumstances before modifying a final custody order. The moving parent must show that modification is in the child's best interests and, in many cases, that the child's present environment endangers physical or emotional health or impairs emotional development. The standard is intentionally demanding to protect stability. Parenting-time adjustments may be easier to obtain than a full custody transfer.
Minnesota.relocation_rules: A parent with whom the child lives may not move the child's residence to another state without consent or court approval, except in limited circumstances. Minnesota courts consider the reason for the move, the parent's relationship with the child, the effect on the child's relationship with the other parent, and whether the move is in the child's best interests. Domestic violence can affect who bears the burden of proof. The court can revise parenting time if a move is approved.
Minnesota.enforcement_options: Minnesota courts enforce custody and parenting time orders through contempt, compensatory parenting time, attorney fees, and related relief. Repeated denial of parenting time can lead to sanctions and may support later custody modification. Courts may also use expedited remedies in some parenting-time disputes. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Minnesota.mediation_requirements: Minnesota courts strongly encourage alternative dispute resolution in family law matters. Many custody disputes go through mediation, early neutral evaluation, or similar court-connected processes before trial. Courts can excuse ADR when there is domestic abuse or another good reason. Parenting classes and custody evaluations are also common tools in contested cases.

Mississippi.quick_summary: Mississippi applies the child's best interests standard and often analyzes custody through the Albright factors. Joint custody is available, but courts still pay close attention to continuity of care, moral fitness, stability, and any history of abuse.
Mississippi.custody_standard: Mississippi courts decide custody based on the best interests of the child, often using the Albright factors from state case law. These factors include the child's age, health, and sex; continuity of care before separation; parenting skills and willingness to provide care; employment responsibilities; physical and mental health; emotional ties; moral fitness; home, school, and community record; and the preference of a child of sufficient age. Domestic violence and abuse are especially important considerations. No parent automatically wins based on gender alone.
Mississippi.custody_types: Mississippi recognizes legal custody and physical custody, each of which may be sole or joint. Joint legal custody means both parents share major decisions, while joint physical custody means the child spends significant periods with both parents. Courts can also award one form jointly and the other solely. Parenting plans often address school decisions, health care, holidays, and transportation.
Mississippi.modification_rules: A Mississippi custody order may be modified when there has been a material change in circumstances adversely affecting the child and the proposed change is in the child's best interests. This is a demanding standard designed to protect stability. Courts usually want proof that the child is being harmed or materially affected by the current arrangement, not just that another arrangement might be preferable. Repeated interference, unsafe behavior, or substantial instability can support modification.
Mississippi.relocation_rules: Mississippi relocation disputes are resolved through the best interests framework and the effect of the move on the child. A move that significantly affects visitation will often trigger court review. Judges consider the reason for the move, the impact on the child's relationship with the other parent, and whether a revised schedule can preserve meaningful contact. Relocation may justify changing custody if the move materially harms the child or undermines the existing arrangement.
Mississippi.enforcement_options: Mississippi courts enforce custody orders through contempt proceedings, attorney fee awards, makeup visitation, and other family court remedies. Persistent violations can also support later modification. Courts may use law enforcement assistance when necessary to return a child. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Mississippi.mediation_requirements: Mississippi courts may order mediation in contested custody disputes. Mediation is not mandatory in every case statewide, but many chancery courts use it before trial. The court can tailor mediation orders to the needs of the case. Abuse allegations may lead the court to avoid or limit traditional mediation.

Missouri.quick_summary: Missouri courts decide custody using the best interests of the child standard and require a written parenting plan in contested cases. Both joint and sole legal and physical custody are available, with strong attention to each parent's willingness to support the child's relationship with the other parent.
Missouri.custody_standard: Missouri courts determine custody according to the best interests of the child. Statutory factors include the wishes of the parents and child, the child's need for frequent and meaningful contact with both parents, the ability of the parents to perform parenting functions, the child's adjustment to home and school, and any history of abuse. Courts also evaluate which parent is more likely to allow the child frequent and continuing contact with the other parent. The analysis is detailed and fact-specific.
Missouri.custody_types: Missouri recognizes legal custody and physical custody, each of which may be joint or sole. Joint legal custody allows parents to share major decisions, while joint physical custody provides substantial time with both parents. Sole custody is available when shared arrangements would not serve the child's best interests. A parenting plan is required and must address decision-making, schedules, transportation, and dispute resolution.
Missouri.modification_rules: Missouri allows modification of custody when facts have arisen since the prior judgment, or were unknown at the time, and a change has occurred in the circumstances of the child or custodian such that modification is necessary to serve the child's best interests. Courts do not change custody lightly. Stability and continuity remain important, especially after a child has adjusted to an established arrangement. Lesser schedule changes may sometimes be handled as parenting-plan adjustments rather than full custody transfers.
Missouri.relocation_rules: Missouri has a specific relocation statute requiring written notice before a child's principal residence is changed. The relocating parent generally must give at least 60 days written notice, including the proposed address and revised schedule. The other parent may object, and the court then decides whether relocation is in the child's best interests. Judges consider the reason for the move and the feasibility of preserving the child's relationship with the nonmoving parent.
Missouri.enforcement_options: Missouri courts enforce custody orders through contempt, attorney fees, compensatory time, and other family court remedies. Repeated denial of custody or visitation rights can lead to sanctions and may support modification. Courts may also issue orders directing law enforcement assistance in appropriate cases. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Missouri.mediation_requirements: Missouri family courts frequently use mediation in contested custody matters. Many circuits require parents to attempt mediation before a trial date is set. Mediation can help parents create or revise parenting plans, but courts may waive it where domestic violence or intimidation makes mediation unsafe. Parent education programs may also be ordered.

Montana.quick_summary: Montana allocates a parenting plan based on the child's best interests and does not assume one parent should receive primary custody by default. Courts look carefully at caregiving history, stability, and whether each parent will support the child's relationship with the other parent.
Montana.custody_standard: Montana courts determine parenting arrangements based on the best interests of the child. Statutory factors include the wishes of the parents, the wishes of the child when mature enough, the child's relationships with parents and siblings, continuity and stability, the mental and physical health of the people involved, and any history of abuse or violence. Judges also consider whether one parent has knowingly failed to pay birth-related or child-related support when relevant. The court's goal is a practical plan that protects the child and supports healthy parenting.
Montana.custody_types: Montana uses parenting plans rather than relying on traditional custody labels alone. A parenting plan can allocate decision-making, residential time, holidays, transportation, and dispute-resolution procedures. The child may reside primarily with one parent or spend substantial time with both. Courts can assign joint or separate authority over major decisions depending on what works best for the child.
Montana.modification_rules: A Montana parenting plan may be modified when there is a change in circumstances and modification is necessary to serve the child's best interests. The state also imposes additional thresholds for some motions filed soon after the prior order. Courts give weight to stability and will not reopen parenting arrangements for minor disputes. Safety concerns, persistent interference, or major life changes can justify review.
Montana.relocation_rules: Montana does not use one universal relocation formula, but a move that substantially changes the parenting plan can be challenged in court. Judges examine the reason for the move, the effect on the child's schooling and community ties, and whether meaningful contact with the other parent can be preserved. The child's best interests remain controlling. If necessary, the court may revise the parenting plan to address distance and travel.
Montana.enforcement_options: Montana courts enforce parenting plans through contempt, compensatory time, attorney fees, and other remedial orders. A parent who repeatedly violates the plan may face sanctions and later custody consequences. Courts can also order law enforcement assistance when a child is being wrongfully withheld. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Montana.mediation_requirements: Montana courts often encourage mediation in parenting disputes, especially when parents need help finalizing or revising a parenting plan. Some districts use mediation routinely before trial, while others refer cases individually. Courts may limit mediation where domestic violence is present. Parenting evaluators and guardian ad litem appointments are also available when needed.

Nebraska.quick_summary: Nebraska resolves custody and parenting time under the best interests of the child standard. Courts may order sole or joint legal and physical custody, but they focus on safety, stability, and the practicality of any shared parenting arrangement.
Nebraska.custody_standard: Nebraska courts decide custody based on the best interests of the child. Judges consider the relationship of the child to each parent, the child's general health and welfare, the child's social behavior, the parents' ability to provide care, and any credible evidence of abuse. Courts also evaluate whether the proposed arrangement encourages a healthy relationship with both parents when that can be done safely. The best interests analysis remains individualized in every case.
Nebraska.custody_types: Nebraska recognizes legal custody and physical custody, each of which may be sole or joint. Joint legal custody allows shared major decisions, while joint physical custody provides substantial residential time with both parents. Courts will award shared arrangements only when they are workable and consistent with the child's best interests. Parenting plans must address schedules, decision-making, transportation, and methods for resolving future disputes.
Nebraska.modification_rules: A Nebraska custody order may be modified when there has been a material change in circumstances affecting the child's best interests. Courts require a real change in facts, not just ongoing frustration between the adults. Stability carries significant weight once a final order is in place. Parenting-time changes may sometimes be granted without a full shift in custody.
Nebraska.relocation_rules: Nebraska relocation disputes are addressed through case law and the child's best interests. A parent who seeks to move with the child in a way that disrupts the existing parenting plan can expect court review if the move is contested. Judges consider whether the move is being pursued in good faith, the impact on the child's relationship with the other parent, and whether an alternate schedule can preserve meaningful contact. Relocation can result in changes to custody or parenting time.
Nebraska.enforcement_options: Nebraska courts enforce custody and parenting time orders through contempt, attorney fees, and compensatory time. Willful denial of court-ordered contact can lead to sanctions and may affect later custody rulings. Courts can also order immediate return of a child in serious circumstances. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Nebraska.mediation_requirements: Nebraska courts make regular use of mediation in parenting cases. Mediation may be ordered before a final hearing, particularly when the parents need help creating or revising a parenting plan. Courts can excuse mediation where domestic abuse or other safety concerns make it inappropriate. Parent education classes are also common in custody cases.

New Hampshire.quick_summary: New Hampshire allocates parental rights and responsibilities based on the child's best interests. Courts can approve shared or sole decision-making and residential arrangements, with close attention to communication, stability, and any history of abuse.
New Hampshire.custody_standard: New Hampshire courts determine parental rights and responsibilities based on the best interests of the child. Statutory factors include the child's relationship with each parent, each parent's ability to provide love and guidance, the child's developmental needs, the quality of the child's adjustment to home and school, and any history of abuse or violence. Courts also consider whether each parent can support the child's relationship with the other parent. The child's preference may be considered when mature enough to express it thoughtfully.
New Hampshire.custody_types: New Hampshire uses the terms decision-making responsibility and residential responsibility. Either can be shared or allocated primarily to one parent. Courts often approve parenting plans that divide major decisions while also spelling out the residential schedule in detail. Shared arrangements are possible, but the court will not order them if conflict or safety concerns make them unworkable.
New Hampshire.modification_rules: A final parental rights order may be modified when there has been a substantial change in circumstances and the requested change is in the child's best interests. Courts look for a genuine shift in facts affecting the child, not just ongoing parental friction. Stability matters, especially after a child has adapted to an established routine. Emergency orders are available when a child's safety is in question.
New Hampshire.relocation_rules: New Hampshire has statutory procedures for relocation when a parent plans to move in a way that significantly affects the parenting plan. The moving parent generally must provide advance notice, and the other parent can object. Courts then decide whether relocation is consistent with the child's best interests, taking into account the reason for the move and the effect on the child's relationship with the nonmoving parent. The parenting plan may be revised if a move is approved.
New Hampshire.enforcement_options: New Hampshire courts enforce parenting orders through contempt, attorney fee awards, compensatory parenting time, and related relief. Repeated violations can support later modification. Courts may also issue emergency or return orders if a child is wrongfully withheld. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
New Hampshire.mediation_requirements: New Hampshire family courts frequently use mediation in parenting disputes. Many cases are referred to mediation or another dispute-resolution process before a final hearing. Mediation can be waived or modified where domestic violence is present. Courts may also order parenting classes, neutral evaluations, or guardian ad litem involvement.

New Mexico.quick_summary: New Mexico decides custody through the best interests of the child standard and often uses parenting plans to allocate timesharing and decision-making. Joint custody is possible, but courts focus on cooperation, safety, and whether the plan is realistic for the child.
New Mexico.custody_standard: New Mexico courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. Judges consider the child's needs, the child's relationship with each parent, each parent's caregiving ability, adjustment to home and school, and any evidence of domestic violence or abuse. Courts also weigh whether a parent will support the child's relationship with the other parent when safe. The child's wishes may be considered if the child is sufficiently mature.
New Mexico.custody_types: New Mexico recognizes legal custody and physical custody, and both can be joint or sole. Courts may order joint legal custody when parents can share major decisions responsibly, while physical custody can be primary with one parent or shared between both. Parenting plans usually cover school-year schedules, holidays, transportation, and dispute-resolution methods. A court can divide decision-making authority by topic when needed.
New Mexico.modification_rules: A custody order may be modified when there has been a substantial and material change in circumstances affecting the welfare of the child. The parent seeking change must show why the existing order no longer serves the child's best interests. Courts do not revise final orders for minor conflict alone. Stability remains an important part of the analysis.
New Mexico.relocation_rules: New Mexico relocation disputes are handled through the best interests standard and the facts of the existing parenting plan. A move that materially affects the other parent's contact with the child may require court involvement. Judges consider the reason for the move, the impact on the child's routine and relationships, and whether a revised schedule can preserve meaningful contact. A court may modify custody or timesharing when relocation changes what is workable.
New Mexico.enforcement_options: New Mexico courts enforce custody orders through contempt, attorney fees, makeup time, and other family court remedies. A parent who repeatedly denies contact can face sanctions and may undermine future custody arguments. Courts may also issue emergency relief when a child is being wrongfully withheld. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
New Mexico.mediation_requirements: New Mexico courts regularly use mediation, settlement facilitation, and parenting conferences in custody cases. Some districts require a dispute-resolution process before a contested trial. Mediation may be bypassed or handled differently in cases involving domestic violence. Courts may also order custody evaluations or the appointment of a guardian ad litem.

North Dakota.quick_summary: North Dakota awards decision-making responsibility and residential responsibility based on the child's best interests. Courts do not presume equal time, but they generally support strong relationships with both parents when that can be done safely.
North Dakota.custody_standard: North Dakota courts determine parental rights and responsibilities according to the best interests of the child. Statutory factors include love and emotional ties, each parent's ability to provide care and guidance, the child's developmental needs, stability of the home, moral fitness, mental and physical health, and any history of domestic violence. The court may consider the child's preference when the child is mature enough. The analysis remains highly individualized.
North Dakota.custody_types: North Dakota uses the terms decision-making responsibility and residential responsibility. Either may be shared or allocated primarily to one parent. Courts often adopt detailed parenting plans that spell out major decisions, weekly schedules, holidays, communication, and transportation. Shared arrangements are available, but only when they are workable and in the child's best interests.
North Dakota.modification_rules: Modification of a custody order generally requires a material change in circumstances and a showing that the requested change is in the child's best interests. North Dakota also has time-based thresholds that can affect how much change must be shown depending on when the motion is filed. Courts protect stability and do not alter final orders lightly. Emergency changes are available when the child faces immediate harm.
North Dakota.relocation_rules: North Dakota requires a parent with primary residential responsibility to obtain consent or court approval before relocating the child out of state or to another place that would significantly impair the other parent's time. Courts review the reason for the move, the impact on the child's relationship with the other parent, and whether a revised schedule can preserve meaningful contact. The child's best interests remain the guiding principle. Relocation may justify changes to residential responsibility.
North Dakota.enforcement_options: North Dakota courts enforce parenting orders through contempt, attorney fees, compensatory time, and other remedial orders. Persistent interference with parenting time can support later modification. Courts may also issue return orders when a child is wrongfully withheld. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
North Dakota.mediation_requirements: North Dakota family courts often encourage mediation in contested custody matters. Some cases are referred to mediation or parenting investigation services before trial. Mediation is not always appropriate when abuse or intimidation is involved. Courts may also order parenting classes or evaluations.

Oregon.quick_summary: Oregon applies the best interests of the child standard and uses parenting plans to structure custody and parenting time. Joint custody requires both parents to agree, so contested cases often result in sole legal custody paired with a detailed parenting schedule.
Oregon.custody_standard: Oregon courts determine custody based on the best interests and welfare of the child. Statutory factors include the emotional ties between the child and family members, each parent's interest in and attitude toward the child, the desirability of continuing an existing relationship, any abuse of one parent by the other, the preference for the primary caregiver when appropriate, and the willingness and ability of each parent to facilitate a relationship with the other parent. Safety can outweigh cooperation concerns. The court's focus is the child's practical well-being.
Oregon.custody_types: Oregon recognizes legal custody and parenting time. Legal custody may be sole or joint, but a court cannot impose joint custody unless both parents agree to it. Physical time is governed by a parenting plan that can provide primary residence with one parent or substantial time with both. Parenting plans are expected to address schedules, holidays, transportation, and dispute resolution.
Oregon.modification_rules: An Oregon custody order may be modified when there has been a substantial change in circumstances and modification is in the child's best interests. Courts require a meaningful change affecting the child or the functioning of the parenting plan, not just ordinary conflict. Stability and continuity remain important considerations. Parenting-time changes may sometimes be addressed separately from custody itself.
Oregon.relocation_rules: Oregon relocation disputes are often governed by the existing judgment, which commonly requires notice before a move. A parent who plans to move in a way that substantially affects parenting time can expect court review if the other parent objects. Judges consider the reason for the move, the effect on the child's relationship with the other parent, and whether a revised schedule can preserve meaningful contact. The child's best interests remain controlling.
Oregon.enforcement_options: Oregon courts enforce custody and parenting-time orders through contempt, compensatory time, attorney fee awards, and other remedial orders. Courts can also modify terms if repeated interference shows the current arrangement is not being honored. Emergency enforcement is available in serious cases. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Oregon.mediation_requirements: Oregon courts frequently use mediation in custody and parenting-time disputes. Many circuits require mediation before a contested custody hearing unless there is a reason to waive it. Mediation may be limited or screened out in domestic violence cases. Parent education and other family court services are also common.

Rhode Island.quick_summary: Rhode Island decides custody using the best interests of the child standard without a fixed presumption for one parent. Courts can award joint or sole custody and usually focus on stability, parental fitness, and the child's relationship with each parent.
Rhode Island.custody_standard: Rhode Island courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. Judges consider the child's relationship with each parent, each parent's ability to provide care and guidance, the child's adjustment to home and school, and any evidence of abuse, neglect, or instability. Courts may also consider the child's wishes if the child is old enough and mature enough to express them responsibly. The analysis is broad and fact-specific rather than formulaic.
Rhode Island.custody_types: Rhode Island recognizes legal custody and physical placement, each of which may be sole or joint. Joint legal custody allows both parents to share major decisions, while physical placement can be shared or primarily with one parent. Courts can also separate decision-making authority from residential time. Parenting orders often address holidays, transportation, and methods for handling future disputes.
Rhode Island.modification_rules: A Rhode Island custody order may be modified when there has been a substantial change in circumstances and the requested modification serves the child's best interests. Courts expect a real change affecting the child's welfare before altering a final order. Stability and continuity remain important considerations. Emergency orders are available if a child's safety is threatened.
Rhode Island.relocation_rules: Rhode Island relocation disputes are handled through the best interests standard and the specific terms of the existing order. A move that significantly affects visitation or school placement may require court review if the other parent objects. Judges look at the reason for the move, the child's need for continuity, and whether meaningful contact with the other parent can be preserved. Relocation can justify changes to custody or visitation when needed.
Rhode Island.enforcement_options: Rhode Island courts enforce custody orders through contempt, makeup visitation, attorney fees, and other family court remedies. Repeated noncompliance can also support later modification. Courts may issue emergency orders or direct a child's return in serious cases. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Rhode Island.mediation_requirements: Rhode Island Family Court often encourages mediation in custody and visitation disputes. Some cases are referred to mediation or other family services before a contested hearing. Mediation may be limited or bypassed where domestic violence or coercion is present. Courts may also appoint guardians ad litem or evaluators when necessary.

South Carolina.quick_summary: South Carolina awards custody using the child's best interests as the guiding standard. Courts can order joint or sole custody, but they place strong emphasis on each parent's caregiving history, fitness, and ability to meet the child's daily needs.
South Carolina.custody_standard: South Carolina courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. Judges consider the child's needs, each parent's parenting ability, the child's relationship with each parent, stability of each home, school and community ties, and any history of abuse, neglect, or substance misuse. The court may consider the child's preference when the child has enough age and maturity for the preference to be meaningful. No single factor controls the result.
South Carolina.custody_types: South Carolina recognizes legal custody and physical custody, each of which may be sole or joint. Joint custody is possible, but courts do not assume it is appropriate in every case. Physical custody may be primary with one parent or shared according to a detailed schedule. Courts often approve parenting plans that address decision-making, holidays, transportation, and communication.
South Carolina.modification_rules: A final custody order may be modified when there has been a substantial change in circumstances affecting the welfare of the child and the requested change is in the child's best interests. South Carolina courts require a meaningful shift in facts rather than routine disagreement. Stability carries significant weight once a child has adjusted to an arrangement. Emergency relief is available when a child's safety is at risk.
South Carolina.relocation_rules: South Carolina relocation disputes are decided under the best interests standard. A parent who plans to move in a way that materially affects the existing visitation schedule should expect court review if the move is contested. Judges consider the reason for the move, the impact on the child's relationship with the other parent, and whether alternate contact arrangements can preserve meaningful ties. A relocation can justify changes to custody or visitation when appropriate.
South Carolina.enforcement_options: South Carolina family courts enforce custody and visitation orders through contempt, attorney fees, makeup time, and related remedies. A parent who repeatedly violates an order can face sanctions and may weaken future custody arguments. Courts may also order counseling or other corrective measures. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
South Carolina.mediation_requirements: South Carolina courts frequently require mediation in family court cases, including contested custody matters, before trial. Mediation is intended to help parents resolve parenting disputes and narrow issues. Courts can excuse or adapt mediation where domestic violence makes standard mediation unsafe. Additional family court services, including guardian ad litem involvement, are also common.

South Dakota.quick_summary: South Dakota applies the best interests of the child standard and does not presume a single custody model fits every family. Courts can award joint or sole custody, but they focus on the child's welfare, continuity, and each parent's ability to cooperate.
South Dakota.custody_standard: South Dakota courts determine custody according to the best interests of the child. Judges consider the child's welfare, the child's relationship with each parent, the stability of each home, moral fitness, and any evidence of abuse or neglect. The child's preference may be considered when the child is mature enough. The court aims to place the child in the arrangement most likely to support healthy development and stability.
South Dakota.custody_types: South Dakota recognizes legal custody and physical custody, each of which may be sole or joint. Joint custody is available when it serves the child's best interests and the parents can cooperate enough to make it work. Courts may instead award primary physical custody to one parent with parenting time to the other. Parenting plans generally address major decisions, weekly schedules, holidays, and transportation.
South Dakota.modification_rules: A South Dakota custody order may be modified when there has been a substantial change in circumstances and modification is in the child's best interests. Courts require more than ordinary disagreement before disturbing a final order. Stability remains important once a child has become established in a routine. Urgent safety issues can support temporary emergency relief.
South Dakota.relocation_rules: South Dakota relocation disputes are handled through the best interests standard and the realities of the current parenting schedule. A move that substantially affects the other parent's time will often lead to court review if contested. Judges consider the reason for the move, the effect on the child's schooling and relationships, and whether meaningful contact with the other parent can be preserved. A relocation may result in modifications to custody or visitation.
South Dakota.enforcement_options: South Dakota courts enforce custody and visitation orders through contempt, attorney fee awards, makeup time, and related family court remedies. Repeated noncompliance can also support later custody modification. Courts may issue return orders in serious withholding cases. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
South Dakota.mediation_requirements: South Dakota courts may order mediation in custody disputes, especially when settlement may be possible before trial. Mediation is not universally mandatory in every case statewide, but judges often use it as a case-management tool. It may be limited or excused where abuse or coercion is alleged. Parenting classes or evaluations may also be ordered.

Tennessee.quick_summary: Tennessee uses the best interests of the child standard and requires a permanent parenting plan in custody cases. Courts do not presume equal time, but they generally want children to have the maximum participation possible with both parents consistent with the child's welfare.
Tennessee.custody_standard: Tennessee courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child using statutory factors. Those factors include each parent's ability to provide care, the strength and stability of the child's relationship with each parent, continuity in the child's life, emotional needs, moral and physical fitness, and any history of abuse. Courts also consider which parent has performed the greater share of parenting responsibilities. The statute specifically directs courts to consider each parent's willingness to facilitate a relationship with the other parent when safe.
Tennessee.custody_types: Tennessee uses the concepts of primary residential parent, alternative residential parent, and decision-making authority under a permanent parenting plan. Major decisions may be made jointly or by one parent depending on the child's needs and the parents' ability to cooperate. The child may reside primarily with one parent or spend substantial time with both. Every final order should include a parenting plan covering schedules, transportation, holidays, and dispute resolution.
Tennessee.modification_rules: Tennessee distinguishes between modifying the residential schedule and changing the primary residential parent. A change in the schedule generally requires a material change in circumstance affecting the child's best interests. Changing the primary residential parent requires a material change affecting the child and a best-interests analysis under the statutory factors. Courts do not alter parenting plans casually because continuity matters.
Tennessee.relocation_rules: Tennessee has a detailed parental relocation statute. A parent spending the greater interval of time with the child generally must give written notice before relocating. If the other parent objects, the court reviews the move under statutory standards that consider the reason for the relocation, the threat of specific and serious harm, and the child's best interests. Approved relocations often lead to revised parenting schedules.
Tennessee.enforcement_options: Tennessee courts enforce parenting plans through contempt, attorney fees, makeup time, and other family court remedies. Repeated violations can support modifications or additional restrictions. Courts may also issue restraining or return orders when a child is wrongfully withheld. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Tennessee.mediation_requirements: Tennessee courts frequently require mediation in contested parenting matters before trial. Mediation is built into many domestic relations case-management processes. Courts may waive or adjust mediation in cases involving domestic abuse or other safety concerns. Parent education seminars are also commonly required.

Utah.quick_summary: Utah determines custody using the child's best interests and has expanded tools for joint legal and joint physical custody when they are workable. Courts examine practical caregiving, communication, and safety rather than assuming one model is right for every family.
Utah.custody_standard: Utah courts determine custody according to the best interests of the child. Statutory factors include the parents' ability to prioritize the child's welfare, the depth and quality of the child's relationship with each parent, previous caregiving responsibilities, the child's adjustment to home and school, and any history of domestic violence or abuse. Courts also evaluate whether parents can communicate and cooperate. The best interests analysis remains individualized and child-centered.
Utah.custody_types: Utah recognizes legal custody and physical custody, each of which may be sole or joint. Joint legal custody is common when parents can share major decisions, and Utah also allows several forms of joint physical custody depending on the number of overnights and the parenting schedule. Courts can also award one parent primary physical custody with parent-time to the other. Parenting plans usually address schedules, holidays, transportation, and decision-making procedures.
Utah.modification_rules: Utah generally requires a material and substantial change in circumstances before modifying a custody order. After that threshold is met, the court decides whether the requested modification is in the child's best interests. Courts do not change final custody orders for minor disagreements alone. Different standards may apply to narrower parent-time adjustments.
Utah.relocation_rules: Utah has statutory notice requirements when a parent with the child for the majority of overnights plans to move a substantial distance. The moving parent generally must give notice, and the other parent can seek court review if the move would affect parent-time. Judges consider the reason for the move, the impact on the child, and whether meaningful contact with the nonmoving parent can be preserved. A revised parenting plan is often required when relocation is approved.
Utah.enforcement_options: Utah courts enforce custody and parent-time orders through contempt, compensatory time, attorney fees, and related family court remedies. Persistent interference can support later modification or additional restrictions. Courts may also issue expedited relief in serious withholding cases. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Utah.mediation_requirements: Utah courts commonly require mediation before a contested custody trial. Mediation is a routine part of domestic case management, though exceptions may apply in cases involving domestic violence or other safety concerns. Parent education and orientation courses are also frequently required. Courts may order evaluations or the appointment of professionals in higher-conflict cases.

Vermont.quick_summary: Vermont allocates parental rights and responsibilities according to the child's best interests. Courts can approve shared or sole arrangements, but they focus on each parent's caregiving role, communication, and the child's need for stability.
Vermont.custody_standard: Vermont courts determine parental rights and responsibilities using the best interests of the child standard. Statutory factors include the child's relationship with each parent, each parent's ability to meet the child's present and future developmental needs, the ability of the parents to communicate and cooperate, the quality of the child's adjustment to home and school, and any evidence of abuse. Courts also consider which parent has been the primary caregiver when relevant. The analysis is practical and child-focused.
Vermont.custody_types: Vermont uses the terms legal responsibility, physical responsibility, and parent-child contact. These responsibilities may be shared or allocated primarily to one parent. Courts often approve detailed parent-child contact schedules that address regular time, holidays, transportation, and communication. Shared arrangements are possible when they are workable and support the child's best interests.
Vermont.modification_rules: A Vermont custody order may be modified when there has been a real, substantial, and unanticipated change in circumstances and modification is in the child's best interests. Courts place real weight on finality and stability, so the change must be meaningful. Ordinary parenting disagreements generally are not enough. Emergency relief is available when the child faces immediate harm.
Vermont.relocation_rules: Vermont relocation disputes are resolved through the best interests standard and the effect on parent-child contact. A move that significantly alters the existing schedule will often require court review if it is contested. Judges consider the reason for the move, its impact on the child's routine and relationships, and whether meaningful contact with the other parent can still be maintained. The court may revise responsibilities and contact terms to account for distance.
Vermont.enforcement_options: Vermont courts enforce parental rights and responsibilities orders through contempt, compensatory contact, attorney fee awards, and related remedies. Repeated violations can also support later modification. Courts can issue return orders when a child is wrongfully withheld. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Vermont.mediation_requirements: Vermont family courts regularly encourage mediation in custody and parent-child contact disputes. Many cases use mediation or another dispute-resolution process before trial. Courts may modify or avoid mediation where abuse or coercive control is present. Parent education, evaluators, and guardians ad litem may also be used where helpful.

West Virginia.quick_summary: West Virginia uses a best interests framework and typically requires a parenting plan covering custodial responsibility and decision-making. Courts may award shared or primary responsibility, but they focus on caregiving history, the child's needs, and any abuse or safety concerns.
West Virginia.custody_standard: West Virginia courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. Judges consider the child's emotional ties, continuity of care, each parent's ability to provide for the child's needs, the stability of each household, and any history of domestic violence, abuse, or substance misuse. Courts also evaluate which arrangement is most likely to promote healthy development and preserve important relationships. The child's wishes may be considered when the child is mature enough to express a reasoned preference.
West Virginia.custody_types: West Virginia uses terms such as custodial responsibility and decision-making responsibility rather than relying solely on older custody labels. Responsibility may be shared or allocated primarily to one parent. Parenting plans address regular schedules, holidays, transportation, and methods for resolving future disputes. Courts can separate major decision-making from residential time when that best fits the family.
West Virginia.modification_rules: A parenting order may be modified when there has been a substantial change in circumstances and the requested modification is in the child's best interests. West Virginia also has statutory provisions that can apply differently depending on how much time has passed since the prior order. Courts do not revisit final orders lightly because stability matters for children. Urgent safety concerns can justify temporary emergency relief.
West Virginia.relocation_rules: West Virginia law expects parents to address relocation through notice and, when contested, court review. A parent planning to move in a way that significantly changes the parenting plan should expect the court to evaluate the reason for the move, the impact on the child's relationship with the other parent, and whether meaningful contact can be preserved. The child's best interests remain controlling. Relocation may support changes to custodial responsibility or visitation terms.
West Virginia.enforcement_options: West Virginia courts enforce parenting orders through contempt, attorney fees, compensatory time, and other family court remedies. Repeated interference with parenting time can also support later modification. Courts may issue immediate return or pick-up orders when necessary. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
West Virginia.mediation_requirements: West Virginia family courts often use mediation in parenting disputes. Many contested cases are referred to mediation or other family court services before a final evidentiary hearing. Mediation may be waived or adapted where domestic violence or coercive behavior makes ordinary mediation unsafe. Parent education and other supportive services may also be ordered.

Wisconsin.quick_summary: Wisconsin awards legal custody and physical placement according to the child's best interests and generally seeks meaningful time with both parents when safe. Courts rely on parenting plans and county family-court services to shape workable schedules in contested cases.
Wisconsin.custody_standard: Wisconsin courts determine legal custody and physical placement based on the best interests of the child. Statutory factors include the wishes of the parents and child, the child's relationship with parents and others, adjustment to home, school, religion, and community, the need for regularly occurring and meaningful placement, and any history of abuse or domestic violence. Courts also consider each parent's ability to cooperate and support the child's relationship with the other parent when safe. No parent is preferred based solely on gender.
Wisconsin.custody_types: Wisconsin recognizes legal custody and physical placement. Legal custody may be joint or sole, and physical placement may be shared or primarily with one parent. Wisconsin frequently uses detailed placement schedules rather than simple labels. Parenting plans and placement proposals commonly address holidays, school breaks, transportation, communication, and decision-making.
Wisconsin.modification_rules: Wisconsin generally requires a substantial change in circumstances before modifying a final custody or placement order. Additional statutory rules can apply depending on how long the order has been in place and whether the request concerns custody or placement. Courts protect stability and expect a meaningful reason to alter a functioning order. Endangerment or persistent interference can support more immediate action.
Wisconsin.relocation_rules: Wisconsin has statutory notice requirements when a parent intends to move a significant distance with the child. A parent planning to move more than a set mileage threshold or out of state generally must give notice, and the other parent may object. If the move is contested, the court evaluates the child's best interests, the reason for the move, and how the child's relationship with the other parent can be preserved. Relocation can result in modified placement schedules.
Wisconsin.enforcement_options: Wisconsin courts enforce placement orders through contempt, compensatory placement time, attorney fees, and related remedies. Repeated denial of placement can support sanctions and may influence later custody or placement decisions. County enforcement services may also be involved in some cases. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Wisconsin.mediation_requirements: Wisconsin counties commonly require mediation through family court services before a contested custody or placement hearing. Mediation is a routine part of the family court process, though abuse cases may be screened out or handled differently for safety. Courts may also order custody studies, guardian ad litem appointments, or parent education. Local county practice shapes the exact process.

Wyoming.quick_summary: Wyoming uses the best interests of the child standard and can award joint or sole legal and physical custody. Courts focus on the child's welfare, parental fitness, and whether the proposed arrangement is realistic and stable.
Wyoming.custody_standard: Wyoming courts determine custody based on the best interests of the child. Judges consider the quality of the child's relationship with each parent, each parent's ability to provide care, the child's adjustment to home and school, and any history of abuse, neglect, or substance misuse. Courts also examine each parent's willingness to allow the child continuing contact with the other parent when safe. The analysis is practical and strongly focused on the child's welfare.
Wyoming.custody_types: Wyoming recognizes legal custody and physical custody, each of which may be sole or joint. Joint arrangements are possible when parents can cooperate and the plan is workable for the child. Courts may instead place primary physical custody with one parent and provide parenting time to the other. Parenting orders often include detailed terms on schedules, holidays, transportation, and decision-making.
Wyoming.modification_rules: A Wyoming custody order may be modified when there has been a material change in circumstances and the requested change is in the child's best interests. Courts require a real change affecting the child's welfare and will not disturb a final order for minor conflict alone. Stability and continuity remain important considerations. Temporary emergency relief is available when a child's safety is threatened.
Wyoming.relocation_rules: Wyoming relocation disputes are handled through the best interests standard and the practical effect of the move on the existing parenting schedule. A parent who plans to move in a way that significantly affects the other parent's contact should expect court review if the move is contested. Judges consider the reason for the move, the impact on the child's relationships and schooling, and whether meaningful contact with the other parent can be preserved. Relocation may support changes to custody or visitation when needed.
Wyoming.enforcement_options: Wyoming courts enforce custody and visitation orders through contempt, makeup time, attorney fee awards, and other remedial orders. Persistent violations can also support later modification. Courts may issue return orders or other emergency relief if a child is wrongfully withheld. Interstate enforcement follows the UCCJEA.
Wyoming.mediation_requirements: Wyoming courts may order mediation in contested custody matters, and many judges encourage it before trial. Mediation is not mandatory in every case statewide, but it is a common settlement tool. Courts may avoid or modify mediation when domestic violence or intimidation is present. Guardians ad litem or custody evaluators may also be appointed when needed.
